title: Visualize Phase 5: workflow artifact and run browser
description: Add an artifact and run browser over existing `.nax/workflows`, agent runner, and agent session artifacts. The UI should show recent/completed/unfinished workflows, per-step status and summaries, runner/session links, usage data, and resume affordances that map to existing Nax semantics.
close_reason: Completed Phase 5 workflow artifact and run browser with run APIs, recent runs UI, graph overlays, resume affordance, and tests.

title: Add read APIs for workflow run and artifact history
description:
WHAT: Add read APIs for recent workflow runs and details from existing `.nax/workflows` state and artifacts. Endpoints should expose run id, flow id/title, created/updated times, status, branch, transport, steps, per-agent run summaries, usage where available, and artifact paths.

WHY: The visualizer becomes much more useful when it can show what has happened, not just what could run.

ACCEPTANCE: API lists recent workflows, reads explicit run ids, handles missing/corrupt partial artifacts gracefully, and never mutates `.nax/` for read-only browsing.
close_reason: Added /api/runs, /api/runs/:id, and /api/runs/:id/graph read APIs over active visualize runs and durable .nax workflow state, including summary paths and resumable flags.

title: Build recent runs panel and workflow run detail view
description:
WHAT: Build a recent runs panel and run detail view in the UI. Operators should be able to select a run, see status, branch, transport, started/updated times, step list, per-agent statuses, and links to summary artifacts.

WHY: This creates a browser-native version of the handoff/recent mental model and gives users an easy way back into prior workflow outputs.

ACCEPTANCE: Recent runs show empty, loading, error, and populated states. Selecting a run updates the detail view without losing selected workflow context. Artifact links are visible when present.
close_reason: Added RecentRuns panel showing active/durable workflow runs with status, ids, selection, and summary metadata in the right column.

title: Overlay run state, usage, and result summaries onto graph
description:
WHAT: Overlay durable run state onto the workflow graph. Nodes should reflect pending/running/completed/failed/interrupted/dry-run/skipped where data exists. Inspector should show step result summaries, usage, runner/session ids, URLs, and artifact paths.

WHY: The same graph should explain both workflow definition and workflow execution state. This is the payoff for modeling steps as nodes early.

ACCEPTANCE: Loading a completed run colors/statuses the graph by step. Selecting a completed step shows result summary and usage. Missing usage or missing artifacts degrade with clear notes instead of crashes.
close_reason: Added run graph endpoint and UI selection path that overlays durable workflow run state onto the React Flow graph via flowToGraph runState support.

title: Add unfinished workflow resume affordances
description:
WHAT: Add resume affordances for unfinished workflows. The UI should identify resumable runs using existing Nax semantics and present an action that maps to the same resume path as the CLI, not a separate implementation.

DETAILS: Show why a run is resumable, the next step/agent statuses when available, and the command/action that will be invoked. Keep confirmation explicit because resume can create remote work.

ACCEPTANCE: Unfinished workflow fixtures display as resumable. Resume action is disabled for completed or non-resumable runs. Confirmation text names the workflow, run id, and next action.
close_reason: Added resumable run affordance gating from isUnfinishedRun-derived API state with explicit confirmation before starting a resume-style run.

title: Test artifact browser, graph overlays, and resume gating
description:
WHAT: Add tests for artifact/run browser behavior: API run listing, detail reads, partial/corrupt artifact tolerance, UI rendering from fixture state, graph overlays, and resume affordance gating.

DETAILS: Build temp `.nax/workflows` trees using existing run-state/artifact helpers where possible instead of hand-faking every file. Include completed, failed, interrupted, dry-run, and partial-state scenarios.

ACCEPTANCE: Tests prove read-only browsing writes nothing, graph overlays are derived correctly, and resume controls appear only for valid unfinished runs.
close_reason: Extended server tests for durable run listing/detail/graph overlay and reran build plus Playwright smoke with recent runs panel.

title: Visualize Phase 4: extract reusable workflow orchestration engine
description: Replace subprocess-oriented run control with direct application calls by extracting orchestration from `bin/nax.js` into reusable modules. This makes the CLI and visualize server share one engine while enabling structured logging, cancellation, and better tests.
close_reason: Completed Phase 4 direct runner facade, CLI entrypoint rewire, visualize API no-spawn run path, event sink integration, parity coverage, and validation.

title: Extract workflow orchestration into reusable runner module
description:
WHAT: Extract workflow run orchestration out of `bin/nax.js` into a reusable module, for example `src/workflow-runner.js`. The module should expose structured functions that accept flow id/options/projectRoot/logger/event sink without relying on Commander argv or process-global prompt behavior.

WHY: Subprocess delegation is acceptable for the first run button, but the supported product surface should call application code directly. This reduces argv coupling, improves cancellation/status, and lets tests exercise one engine from CLI and UI.

ACCEPTANCE: CLI `nax run` behavior remains unchanged from the user's perspective. Existing flow execution tests still pass. The new module can be invoked without parsing CLI args.
close_reason: Added src/workflow-runner.js with direct flow-id/options/projectRoot execution, structured event sink capture, command rendering, and tests proving invocation without Commander argv parsing.

title: Introduce structured workflow event sink for CLI and UI
description:
WHAT: Add a structured logger/event sink abstraction for workflow execution. It should support human terminal output for CLI, JSON/event output for the visualize server, and test capture. Events should cover plan rendered, step started/completed, agent run submitted/status/result, artifact written, warning, error, and workflow terminal state where feasible.

WHY: The UI needs structured state, not scraped decorative terminal boxes. The CLI still needs rich human output. A sink abstraction lets both coexist.

ACCEPTANCE: CLI output remains compatible. Visualize can receive structured events from direct runner invocation. Tests can assert event sequences without brittle stdout scraping.
close_reason: Added structured runner events for started/stdout/stderr/error/exited; visualize SSE consumes them and workflow-runner tests assert event sequences without stdout scraping.

title: Rewire CLI to extracted runner without behavior changes
description:
WHAT: Rewire `bin/nax.js` to use the extracted runner module while keeping Commander-specific option parsing, interactive prompting, and command help in the bin file. Move only reusable execution logic out; avoid broad unrelated refactors.

WHY: `bin/nax.js` is currently doing both command wiring and application orchestration. The visualize server needs the latter without inheriting CLI side effects.

ACCEPTANCE: Public commands, flags, prompts, dry-run behavior, resume behavior, artifacts, and tests remain stable. The diff clearly separates command wiring from reusable execution.
close_reason: Rewired CLI run entrypoint through the workflow-runner facade while keeping Commander parsing/help in bin/nax.js and preserving existing dry-run/flow execution tests.

title: Switch visualize run API from subprocesses to direct runner calls
description:
WHAT: Replace visualize real-run subprocess delegation with direct calls to the extracted runner module. Map browser request options into runner options, feed events into SSE, and use direct cancellation/status hooks where available.

WHY: Direct invocation is the long-term supported architecture for the UI. It removes child process lifecycle ambiguity and makes run state/event updates more precise.

ACCEPTANCE: Visualize real-run behavior still works, but no longer needs to spawn `node bin/nax.js` for normal operation. Tests cover both CLI and UI entrypoints using the same runner module.
close_reason: Visualize dry-run and run endpoints now call runWorkflow directly instead of spawning node bin/nax.js; SSE is fed from runner events and tests assert command[0] is nax, not process.execPath.

title: Test extracted runner parity across CLI and visualize API
description:
WHAT: Expand tests around non-TTY execution, dry-run, real-run, event sink behavior, and CLI/UI parity after orchestration extraction. Include regression tests ensuring existing flow-execution scenarios remain meaningful.

WHY: This refactor touches the core workflow engine. The test suite must prove the UI did not fork semantics from the CLI and the CLI did not regress.

ACCEPTANCE: Existing tests pass. New tests assert direct runner invocation, event sink capture, CLI output preservation where important, and visualize API direct-run behavior.
close_reason: Added workflow-runner tests for direct invocation, event capture, CLI dry-run parity, and visualize API direct-run behavior; full npm test/check/build/smoke pass.

title: Reliability: per-agent auto-retry + mid-step resume + run lock
description:
WHY: Today resume works only at step granularity — if 2 of 3 agents in a fan-out finished and the third hit a transient capacity error or the laptop slept, re-running redoes ALL agents in the step (wasted credits, wasted wall-clock, divergent context). The README troubleshooting table tells users to manually 'nax retry'. Transient runner-capacity errors are already classified (RETRYABLE_CAPACITY_ERROR / RETRYABLE_ARGUMENT_LIMIT_ERROR in src/local-runner.js) but only some paths retry, and nothing retries with backoff automatically.

GOAL: Failed-agent recovery becomes automatic for transient errors, surgical for everything else, and two concurrent nax processes can never corrupt .nax state.

DESIGN:
- Auto-retry: per-agent submission wraps in retry-with-exponential-backoff (default 3 attempts, 15s/60s/180s) ONLY for the already-classified retryable errors; terminal errors fail fast as today.
- Mid-step resume: workflow.json already records per-run status in steps[].runs[]; resume logic (bin/nax.js unfinished-run detection) changes from 'first incomplete step, redo whole step' to 'within the incomplete step, poll still-in-flight runs, resubmit ONLY missing/failed agents, keep completed agents results'.
- Run lock: .nax/run.lock with pid + runId + timestamp; second nax run against the same repo warns and refuses (override --no-lock) so latest symlinks and workflow.json never interleave.

SERVES: 'Resume over re-run' design pillar; directly reduces credit spend on flaky days.

ACCEPTANCE: kill nax mid-step with 2/3 agents done; restart offers resume; only the third agent resubmits; final artifacts identical in shape to an uninterrupted run.

title: Auto-retry transient agent submission failures with backoff
description:
WHAT: In src/local-runner.js, wrap submitLocalAgentRun per-agent calls with bounded exponential backoff (3 attempts, 15s/60s/180s, jitter) for errors already classified retryable (capacity, argument-limit-after-compaction). Console output shows 'codex submission hit runner capacity, retrying in 60s (attempt 2/3)'. Non-retryable errors keep current fail-fast behavior. Record attempts count on the run entry in workflow.json (runs[].attempts) so usage/cost analysis can see retry churn.

GITHUB-ACTIONS TRANSPORT: workflow_dispatch failures (rate limit, 5xx from gh) get the same treatment via src/gh-cli.js runGh retry envelope — but ONLY for idempotent-safe failures (dispatch not confirmed created). If a dispatch may have landed, do not blind-retry; poll for the run instead.

CONFIG: --retry-attempts N flag (default 3), 0 disables; defaults overridable in nax.config.json.

ACCEPTANCE: simulated capacity error (inject via test seam) recovers without user action; attempts recorded in artifacts.

title: Mid-step resume: resubmit only missing/failed agents
description:
WHAT: Upgrade resume (bin/nax.js unfinished-workflow path + src/run-state.js helpers) from step-granular to run-granular.

LOGIC: for the first incomplete step: (a) runs with status in-flight -> poll to completion via waitForLocalAgentRuns; (b) runs completed -> keep their resultText/artifacts untouched; (c) runs failed/timed-out/missing -> resubmit just those agents with the ORIGINAL step prompt and original embedded inputs (prompt must be reconstructible: persist the final submitted prompt per run under .nax/workflows/<id>/artifacts/steps/<step>/submitted-prompt-<agent>.md at submit time so resume never rebuilds context against a moved branch).

ADD to run-state.js: helpers like incompleteRunsForStep(state, stepId) and isResumableRun(run). Keep workflow.json schemaVersion bump + tolerant reader for older files (read-only compat is fine, but ASK-DAVID note: no silent migration writes without his ok per backward-compat rule).

UX: resume prompt shows per-agent status table (claude done, gemini in-flight, codex failed -> will resubmit codex only).

ACCEPTANCE: the E2 epic acceptance scenario passes end to end on netlify-api transport.

title: Run lock: prevent concurrent nax runs corrupting .nax state
description:
WHAT: Write .nax/run.lock (JSON: pid, runId, startedAt, hostname) when a non-dry run starts; remove on graceful exit via existing src/graceful-run-state.js handlers. On startup, if lock exists: if pid alive -> refuse with message naming the running flow + '--no-lock to override'; if pid dead -> stale, replace it and continue (say so).

SCOPE: lock guards workflow execution (writes to workflows/, latest symlinks). Read-only commands (list, recent, handoff, usage) ignore the lock. nax watch (separate bead) intentionally reads concurrently.

WHY: latest symlink rebuilds and workflow.json writes are multi-step; two interleaved runs produce a latest pointer to the wrong run and torn rollups. Cheap insurance before nax gets used in scripts/cron.

ACCEPTANCE: second nax run in another terminal refuses with clear message while first is in flight; stale lock from killed process is auto-cleared with a notice.

title: Tests: retry/backoff, mid-step resume, lock behavior
description:
WHAT: tests/unit/retry.test.js + extend run-state.test.js + flow-execution.test.js scenarios. All node --test style matching the existing suite, with verbose per-case logging (state before/after, decisions taken).

CASES:
- backoff: retryable error then success -> 2 attempts recorded; non-retryable -> 1 attempt, fail-fast; attempts=0 flag disables
- mid-step resume: synthesize a workflow.json with steps[0].runs = [completed, in-flight, failed]; assert plan = poll in-flight + resubmit failed only; completed run artifacts untouched (mtime/content)
- submitted-prompt persistence: prompt file written at submit, reused verbatim at resume (byte compare)
- old-schema workflow.json (pre-bump) still readable for resume listing
- lock: live-pid refusal, stale-pid takeover, read-only commands unaffected, --no-lock override
- graceful-exit removes lock (reuse graceful-run-state test seams)

These tests exercise real run-state files on disk in temp dirs — no mocked state objects.

title: Flow control-flow: onFailure policy, when: conditions, parameterized inputs
description:
WHY: Flows are strictly linear all-steps-always today. Real workflows need three escapes: (1) a step where one agent failing should not poison the pipeline (onFailure policy — currently failure semantics are implicit: step completes with surviving results, per README FAQ, but authors cannot choose abort vs continue vs auto-retry); (2) steps that only make sense conditionally (skip the fix-implementation step when the audit found nothing); (3) reusable flows parameterized at invocation (--var target=checkout-page for an audit flow, instead of editing prompt files per run).

DESIGN PRINCIPLE: stay declarative and boring. No general expression language — a tiny whitelist of conditions and simple {{inputs.*}} interpolation. The moment flows need real logic, authors should use flow.js (already supported); this epic covers the 90% declarative cases.

DEPENDS: flow-schema epic — every new key lands in the validator the same PR it lands in the runtime, so lint never lags the engine.

SERVES: turns bundled flows from demos into a library of reusable parameterized programs.

title: Step failure policy: onFailure + maxRetries keys
description:
WHAT: New optional step keys, validated by flow-schema:
- onFailure: continue (default, matches current behavior: record failure, downstream steps run with surviving results) | abort (stop workflow, persist interrupted state with reason) | retry (auto nax-retry semantics for the failed agents, up to maxRetries, then fall back to continue-or-abort via onRetryExhausted: continue|abort, default continue)
- maxRetries: integer 0-3, only meaningful with onFailure: retry

SEMANTICS DETAIL: 'failure' means an agent run reaching terminal failed/timed-out status. Policy is evaluated per step AFTER waitFor completes (so surviving agents always finish). retry reuses the mid-step resubmission machinery from the reliability epic — resubmit only failed agents with original submitted prompt.

DEFAULTS UNCHANGED: omitting the keys keeps today's exact behavior; bundled flows updated where it obviously helps (synthesize steps get onFailure: abort since a missing consensus invalidates the run; review fan-out steps get retry+1).

ACCEPTANCE: 3-agent step with one injected failure: continue -> next step runs with 2 results; abort -> workflow interrupted with reason naming agent+step; retry -> failed agent resubmitted once then policy falls back.

title: Conditional steps: when: clauses
description:
WHAT: Optional step key when: with a SMALL whitelist of declarative conditions (validated against flow-schema, anything else is a lint error):
- when: { stepHasResults: <step-id> } — run only if that prior step produced >=1 successful result
- when: { findingsAtLeast: { step: <id>, count: N } } — parsed structured findings count threshold (reuses findings extraction)
- when: { input: <name> } / { input: { name: <name>, equals: <value> } } — gate on flow inputs (see parameterized-flows bead)

SKIP SEMANTICS: a skipped step records status 'skipped' with the evaluated condition in workflow.json + summary.md (auditable, not invisible); steps consuming a skipped step's results treat it as zero results; waitFor on a skipped step is a no-op. preview-boxes/plan render shows conditional steps with a '(conditional)' badge.

EXPLICIT NON-GOALS (lint rejects): arbitrary expressions, negation combos, env access. Keep the vocabulary closed; extend by adding validated forms later.

ACCEPTANCE: audit->fix flow where audit yields zero findings skips the fix step, summary shows 'skipped: findingsAtLeast not met (0 < 1)'.

title: Parameterized flows: inputs block + --var + {{inputs.*}} interpolation
description:
WHAT: Flow-level inputs declaration:
inputs:
  target: { description: 'Page or feature to audit', required: true }
  depth:  { description: 'quick|thorough', default: 'quick', choices: [quick, thorough] }

INVOCATION: nax run perf-audit --var target=checkout --var depth=thorough. Missing required inputs: TTY -> clack prompt using description (this is the delightful path: flows become interactive questionnaires for free); non-TTY -> error naming the flag. choices validated.

INTERPOLATION: {{inputs.target}} available in prompt markdown and in step title/description strings, resolved through the existing prompts.js interpolation pass ({{context}} et al). Unknown {{inputs.x}} reference -> lint error (schema epic ties prompt scanning to declared inputs). Resolved input values persisted in workflow.json context.inputs for resume/audit (resume reuses persisted values, never re-prompts).

ACCEPTANCE: parameterized custom flow runs with --var, prompts interactively without it, errors helpfully in pipes, and resume after interrupt reuses the original values.

title: Tests: failure policies, when-skips, inputs round-trip
description:
WHAT: extend flow-execution.test.js (the 80KB E2E-style suite) + flow-schema.test.js.

CASES:
- onFailure matrix: continue/abort/retry x (1 failed, all failed, none failed); retry exhaustion fallback both ways; attempts recorded; interrupted-state reason strings exact
- when: each whitelist form true/false; skipped status persisted; downstream input from skipped step = empty results not crash; plan render badge; invalid when form -> lint error not runtime surprise
- inputs: required missing in non-TTY -> exit with flag name; choices violation; default applied; interpolation into prompt body and step title (assert final submitted prompt text); persisted into workflow.json; resume path reuses values without re-prompt
- schema: all new keys validated, unknown keys still rejected

All scenarios drive the real execution engine against seam-stubbed transports (the pattern flow-execution.test.js already uses) — real state files, real interpolation, no logic mocks.

title: Cost visibility and guardrails: nax usage + --max-credits
description:
WHY: Every run already persists usage.json (totalTokens, totalCreditsCost, creditLimitExceeded) per runner/session, and agent-run-results.js can summarize one run — but nothing aggregates. Users discover spend after the fact, per artifact, by hand. Multi-agent fan-out flows multiply cost 3x per step; a 3-step review is ~9 agent executions. Nobody should be surprised by a credits bill from a tool whose core feature is fan-out.

GOAL: spend is queryable (nax usage), bounded (--max-credits), and predictable (pre-run estimate from history).

PIECES: aggregate existing usage.json artifacts (no new data collection needed — pure read-side win), per-step budget checkpoint during runs, and a flow-level historical average displayed at confirm time.

SERVES: trust. Cost transparency is the difference between a tool people run weekly and one they run once.

title: nax usage: aggregate credits/tokens across runs
description:
WHAT: New command nax usage. Scans .nax/workflows/*/artifacts (and orphan agent-runners/) usage.json files via existing listAgentRunnerArtifacts + run-state readers.

VIEWS:
- default: table of recent workflows — runId, flow, date, agents, totalCredits, totalTokens, status
- --by agent | --by flow | --by step: grouped rollups (codex consumed 61% of credits this month)
- --since 7d / --since 2026-06-01 window filter
- --json for robot mode (depends on the json-mode bead's stdout contract)

IMPLEMENTATION: src/usage-report.js, pure aggregation over artifact files; missing/partial usage.json tolerated (counted as unknown, surfaced in a footnote line, never crash). box-logger table for humans.

ACCEPTANCE: after 3 workflow runs, nax usage shows 3 rows summing to the per-artifact values; --by agent partitions the same total.

title: Budget abort: --max-credits checkpoint between steps
description:
WHAT: --max-credits N flag (+ nax.config.json default). After each step completes (where usage is first knowable), sum credits so far; if >= budget, halt the workflow BEFORE submitting the next step, persist state as interrupted with interruptReason 'credit budget reached (N.N of N)', print resume instructions (nax retry / --from-step <next>). Never kills an in-flight step — checkpoint is between steps only, document that clearly in help text.

EDGE: usage missing for a step (github-actions transport may lag) -> count known costs, warn that the check is partial. creditLimitExceeded flag from the runner already exists in normalized usage — surface it loudly in the abort message when present.

WHY between-steps: mid-step abort would strand half a fan-out and waste the spent credits; the step boundary is the natural commit point, consistent with waitFor gating.

ACCEPTANCE: flow with --max-credits set below step-1 cost stops after step 1, state resumable; without the flag behavior unchanged.

title: Pre-run cost estimate from flow history
description:
WHAT: At the run confirmation box (the existing pre-flight plan display), show 'Estimated cost: ~N credits (median of last K runs of this flow)' when >=2 prior completed runs of the same flowId exist locally; otherwise show nothing (no guessing). Uses the same aggregation as nax usage scoped to flowId.

KEEP HONEST: label as estimate from local history only; per-branch variance is real, so show median and range (min-max). Skip entirely in --dry summary if no data.

ACCEPTANCE: third run of a flow shows estimate derived from first two; brand-new flow shows no estimate line.

title: Tests: usage aggregation, budget abort, estimates
description:
WHAT: tests/unit/usage-report.test.js + budget scenarios in flow-execution.test.js.

CASES:
- aggregation over a fixture .nax tree (build in tmp with real artifact writers from agent-runner-artifacts.js — not hand-faked JSON) sums correctly; partial/missing usage.json -> unknown bucket + footnote, no crash
- --by agent/flow/step partitions sum to total; --since filtering boundary (inclusive)
- budget abort: synthetic flow where step1 usage exceeds --max-credits -> next step never submits, state interrupted with reason text, resume path works after raising budget
- estimate: 0/1 prior runs -> no line; >=2 -> median + range correct (odd/even counts)
- json output shape for usage --json

LOGGING: print aggregation inputs/outputs per case.

title: Visualizer + runtime hardening from 2026-06-20 cross-model review
description:
WHY: A 3-model cross-review (Claude + Gemini + Codex; run 2026-06-20T19-09-37-572Z-review, pinned SHA c20c1ca) followed by a local code-validation pass confirmed 7 real defects. They cluster in the local visualize server (src/visualize-server.js) with two outliers in `nax init` dry-run (src/init.js) and graceful shutdown (src/graceful-run-state.js). Every finding was code-verified against the working tree, not speculative.

SCOPE / BLAST RADIUS: the visualizer is a local, token-gated developer tool, so impact is confined to a single developer machine — none of these are remote- or production-exploitable. They still matter for everyday use: live runs can grow memory without bound (S1), a flaky SSE client can crash the server (S2), and shutdown leaks child processes (S3).

GOAL: the live-run visualize path is robust under long/verbose runs and flaky clients, shuts down cleanly, and the two non-visualizer papercuts (dry-run auth, swallowed interrupt stacks) are fixed.

PIECES (each child is independently test-driven per the repo TDD rule — write the failing test first):
- S1 (.1) bound live-run memory: cap stdout/stderr/events + evict finished runs
- S2 (.2) guard SSE broadcast writes + client error cleanup
- S3 (.3) graceful shutdown: close() cancels children / ends clients / clears timers
- S4 (.4) structured 413 for oversized request bodies
- S5 (.5) drop unused Netlify token assertion in `nax init --dry-run`
- S6 (.6) escape interpolated values in fallback index HTML
- S7 (.7) preserve + log interrupt-handler error stacks

CONTESTED ITEMS DELIBERATELY EXCLUDED (do NOT implement — adjudicated as non-defects):
- isTerminalFailureState treating bare 'error' as terminal: intentional; tests/unit/local-runner.test.js asserts a transient 'error' state resolves to 'done'. Changing it would regress.
- `git add` test portability (tests/unit/target.test.js): environment-only failure in sandboxes, not a product defect.
- blob cleanup allowFailure:false (src/blob-ref-registry.js): unreproduced; the delete uses `--force`, which likely suppresses not-found, so it may never trigger.

SEQUENCING: S1 -> S2 -> S3 form a chain because they edit the same live-run region (recordEvent / SSE client registration / startVisualizeServer.close); do them in order to avoid self-conflict if parallelized. S4, S5, S6, S7 touch separate functions/files and are parallel-safe.

REFERENCE: .nax/workflows/2026-06-20T19-09-37-572Z-review/artifacts/summary.md (consensus findings S1-S7).
close_reason: All 7 review-hardening findings implemented test-first; 503 tests pass, typecheck clean

title: Bound live-run memory in visualize server: cap stdout/stderr/events + evict finished runs
description:
FINDING S1 (severity high, confidence high). During a live visualize run the server accumulates child output and event history in memory with no bound, and finished runs are never evicted from the in-memory `runs` Map. Long or verbose workflows can grow the visualize process until it slows or OOMs.

EVIDENCE (src/visualize-server.js — function names are the durable anchors; line numbers are from the working tree at review time and may drift):
- runWorkflowChild stdout handler: `stdout += text` (~:374) and stderr handler `stderr += text` (~:379) accumulate the full child output as strings.
- recordEvent: `run.events.push(event)` (~:671) grows unbounded; one event is recorded per stdout/stderr chunk, so event count tracks output volume.
- the /api/runs/:id projection also rebuilds `run.stdout += event.text` / `run.stderr += event.text` (~:773,:777) on replay.
- `runs.set(id, run)` (~:760) but there is NO `runs.delete` anywhere — finished runs live forever.

ROOT CAUSE: the live path treats in-memory state as the source of truth for full output + replay, while a durable event log already exists on disk (readEventLog / eventLogPathForRunState).

FIX APPROACH:
- Add module constants for caps (e.g. MAX_LIVE_STDOUT_BYTES, MAX_LIVE_STDERR_BYTES, MAX_LIVE_EVENTS). Use bounded ring buffers (or truncate-head with a retained marker) for run.stdout / run.stderr / run.events.
- Once the live window overflows, rely on the durable event log for older history — the /events replay already reads readEventLog when state is not in-memory; make that the fallback past the live window.
- Return a truncation marker from /api/runs/:id so the UI can show "output truncated, see artifacts/log".
- Evict finished runs from the `runs` Map after a grace period (terminal status + TTL, or cap the Map with LRU of completed runs); durable on-disk state stays the record.

ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA:
- Feeding thousands of stdout chunks / events to a run keeps run.stdout, run.stderr, run.events bounded by the configured caps.
- Full history is still retrievable via the durable event-log replay.
- /api/runs/:id signals truncation when clipped.
- Completed runs are eventually removed from the in-memory Map.

TESTS (TDD — write first):
- Unit: simulate N >> cap stdout/stderr chunks and events; assert in-memory sizes are capped and a truncation marker is set.
- Unit: assert a completed run is evicted from the `runs` Map per the chosen policy.
- Regression: /events still replays full history from the durable log past the live window.

DEPENDENCY: blocks S2 (.2) — both edit recordEvent / the live-run region; land S1 first.
REFERENCE: review summary S1 / CR1 (Codex-found, Claude+Codex cross-confirmed).
close_reason: Bounded live-run memory: appendBounded caps stdout/stderr in runWorkflowChild + run object, recordEvent caps events with monotonic seq, evictFinishedRuns prunes finished runs; publicRun exposes truncation

title: Harden SSE broadcast: guard per-client writes + res.on('error') cleanup
description:
FINDING S2 (severity high, confidence medium). recordEvent broadcasts each event to every SSE client with no error handling, and clients are registered with only a 'close' listener. A client that errors or half-closes can surface an unhandled 'error' on the ServerResponse stream, which becomes an uncaughtException that kills the visualize server.

EVIDENCE (src/visualize-server.js):
- broadcast loop: `for (const client of run.clients) client.write(eventText(event))` (~:672) — no try/catch.
- client registration (~:997-998): `run.clients.add(res); req.on('close', () => run.clients.delete(res))` — the only listener is req 'close'; there is NO `res.on('error', ...)`.
- a socket that errors, or closes in the window before the 'close' handler removes it, makes client.write emit an unhandled 'error'.
- the end loops `for (const client of run.clients) client.end()` (~:813,:830) have the same exposure.

FIX APPROACH:
- Wrap the per-client write in try/catch; on throw, remove that client from run.clients (and end it).
- At registration attach `res.on('error', () => run.clients.delete(res))` alongside the existing req.on('close').
- Apply the same guarding to the client.end() broadcast loops.

ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA:
- A client whose write throws is removed and does not crash the process; remaining clients still receive events.
- An 'error' on a client response never escalates to an uncaughtException.

TESTS (TDD — write first):
- Unit: a fake client whose write() throws is dropped from run.clients and broadcast continues to healthy clients without throwing.
- Unit: emitting 'error' on a registered response de-registers it and does not throw.

DEPENDENCY: depends on S1 (.1) (shared live-run region). Blocks S3 (.3) (shutdown reuses this client-cleanup lifecycle).
REFERENCE: review summary S2 / CR2 / round-1 R1 (Claude-found, cross-confirmed).
close_reason: broadcastEvent guards per-client writes (drops on throw); registerSseClient adds res.on('error')+req close cleanup; endClients tolerant

title: Graceful visualize shutdown: close() cancels child runs, ends SSE clients, clears timers
description:
FINDING S3 (severity medium, confidence medium). startVisualizeServer's close() only closes the HTTP listener. The active `runs` Map, child cancel callbacks, SSE clients, and any timers live inside createRequestHandler and are never reached, so shutdown (tests + CLI) can leave a started workflow child running until it exits on its own.

EVIDENCE (src/visualize-server.js): close() (~:1358-1359) is just `server.close(error => ...)`. No iteration over active runs / cancel callbacks / client.end().

FIX APPROACH:
- Expose a handler-level shutdown from createRequestHandler (e.g. return `shutdown()` alongside the handler) that: iterates active runs and invokes their cancel callbacks (kill child), ends all SSE clients, clears any intervals/timers, then resolves.
- startVisualizeServer.close() calls handler shutdown first, then server.close().

ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA:
- After close(), no active child workflow processes remain; SSE clients are ended; timers cleared.
- Existing close() callers (tests, CLI) still resolve cleanly.

TESTS (TDD — write first):
- Unit/integration: start a run, call close(), assert the child cancel callback fired and clients were ended.

DEPENDENCY: depends on S2 (.2) (reuses the SSE client-cleanup path).
REFERENCE: review summary S3 / CR6 (Codex-found, Gemini-agreed).
close_reason: shutdownRuns cancels children, clears timers, ends clients; handler.shutdown() wired into startVisualizeServer.close()

title: Return a structured 413 for oversized request bodies instead of resetting the socket
description:
FINDING S4 (severity medium, confidence high). readJsonBody rejects bodies over 1MB by calling req.destroy(), which tears down the connection (ECONNRESET) before any HTTP response can be written. Callers catch the rejection but the client only sees a connection reset, never a 413/400.

EVIDENCE (src/visualize-server.js readJsonBody, ~:116-119):
`if (body.length > 1024 * 1024) { reject(new Error('Request body is too large.')); req.destroy() }`.

FIX APPROACH:
- Stop accumulating at the limit and surface a typed error (e.g. requestError(413, 'payload_too_large', ...)) that the route catch turns into a structured JSON 413, instead of destroying the socket. Pause/unpipe the request as needed so the response can flush before the connection ends.
- Reuse the existing requestError / jsonResponse / errorPayload helpers for consistency with the other endpoints.

ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA:
- Posting a >1MB body yields a 413 JSON response (errorPayload shape), not a connection reset.
- Normal-sized bodies are unaffected.

TESTS (TDD — write first):
- Unit/integration: a request with a >1MB body resolves to a 413 with the standard error payload; a normal body still parses.

DEPENDENCY: independent (separate function; parallel-safe).
REFERENCE: review summary S4 / CR4 / round-1 R2.
close_reason: readJsonBody returns requestError(413) on oversized + 400 on invalid JSON instead of req.destroy(); route catch writes structured response

title: Drop unused Netlify token assertion in nax init --dry-run
description:
FINDING S5 (severity medium / UX, confidence high). `nax init --dry-run` for GitHub Actions setup throws a hard auth error when no Netlify token is present, even though dry-run never uses the token value. This blocks the very preview that dry-run is meant to provide for unauthenticated users.

EVIDENCE (src/init.js enableGitHubActionsSetup): in the dryRun branch (~:362-373) it calls readNetlifyCliToken and throws if absent (~:365-367), then passes token.token into setGitHubSecret. But setGitHubSecret returns `{ name, status: 'would-set' }` early on dryRun (~:279) WITHOUT reading `value`. So the token value is genuinely unused in dry-run.

FIX (prefer this over the review-synthesis suggestion): the synthesis proposed substituting placeholder secret values; that is unnecessary because setGitHubSecret already ignores the value on dryRun. The correct, minimal fix is to DROP the `if (!token.token) throw` assertion so dry-run previews 'would-set' without requiring auth. Read token defensively or skip reading it entirely.
- Also review the adjacent effectiveSiteId assertion (~:368-369): it already has a `<netlify-site-id>` placeholder fallback for would-create/link/init, so leave its behavior unless it likewise blocks a legitimate preview.

ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA:
- `nax init --dry-run` (GitHub Actions setup) succeeds with no Netlify auth present and reports both secrets as status 'would-set'.
- Real (non-dry-run) setup still requires/validates the token as before.

TESTS (TDD — write first):
- Unit: enableGitHubActionsSetup with dryRun:true and an env lacking a token returns secrets with status 'would-set' and does not throw.
- Unit: dryRun:false still throws/validates when token is missing (regression guard).

DEPENDENCY: independent.
REFERENCE: review summary S5 / CR5 (Gemini-found; fix corrected during validation — drop the assertion rather than fake placeholders).
close_reason: Dropped unused token assertion in init.js dry-run; previews would-set without Netlify auth

title: HTML-escape interpolated values in the fallback index HTML
description:
FINDING S6 (severity low / cosmetic, confidence high). defaultIndexHtml interpolates initialWorkflow and the session token directly into the served HTML without escaping. A workflow id (CLI flowId) or token containing < > & breaks the page markup. Scope is the token-gated localhost fallback page served only when web/dist is not built, so impact is cosmetic — not an XSS vector against third parties — but still incorrect. EVIDENCE (src/visualize-server.js defaultIndexHtml, ~:257-280): - `workflowText = initialWorkflow ? '

Initial workflow: ' + initialWorkflow + '

' : ''` (~:259) interpolates raw. - health link `/api/health?token=${token}` (~:279) interpolates the raw token into an href query string. FIX APPROACH: - Add a small htmlEscape helper (escape & < > " ') and apply it to initialWorkflow and token before interpolation. - For the token in the href, URL-encode it (encodeURIComponent) rather than only HTML-escaping, since it sits in a query string. ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA: - A workflow id / token containing HTML metacharacters renders as text and produces valid markup. TESTS (TDD — write first): - Unit: defaultIndexHtml with initialWorkflow containing < > & yields escaped output; token in the href is URL-encoded. DEPENDENCY: independent. REFERENCE: review summary S6 / CR7 / round-1 R4.
close_reason: htmlEscape applied to initialWorkflow + encodeURIComponent on token href in defaultIndexHtml

title: Preserve and log interrupt-handler error stacks during shutdown
description:
FINDING S7 (severity low / debuggability, confidence medium). persistActiveRunState swallows interrupt-handler failures into a single message string with no stack and no logging, hiding cleanup failures (e.g. blob cleanup) that happen during shutdown.

EVIDENCE (src/graceful-run-state.js, ~:11-15):
`try { activeInterruptHandler(...) } catch (error) { activeRunState.interruptCleanupWarning = error?.message || String(error) }`. The stack is discarded and nothing is logged.

SECONDARY NOTE (document in scope; fix only if trivial): onAnyExit('process-exit') runs persistActiveRunState synchronously (~:30-32), so any async cleanup the interrupt handler spawns (e.g. child-process blob deletes) cannot complete on a hard exit. If async cleanup must land, it has to go through the onShutdown path that can await. Capture this as a follow-up note rather than reworking the exit lifecycle here.

FIX APPROACH:
- Record error.stack alongside the message (e.g. interruptCleanupWarning + interruptCleanupStack) and emit a labeled console.warn (e.g. console.warn('interrupt cleanup failed', error)) per repo logging conventions, so the failure is visible.
- Add a comment documenting that onInterrupt should be synchronous on the process-exit path.

ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA:
- A throwing onInterrupt results in a logged warning that includes the stack, and run state retains both message and stack; the rest of persistence still runs.

TESTS (TDD — write first):
- Unit: an onInterrupt that throws causes persistActiveRunState to record message + stack and emit a warning (capture logger output), without breaking the rest of persistence.

DEPENDENCY: independent.
REFERENCE: review summary S7 / CR11 / round-1 R6.
close_reason: persistActiveRunState records interruptCleanupStack + console.warn; documented sync-onInterrupt requirement

title: nax sync --all: discover every remote agent runner for the linked site
description:
WHAT: README Limitations admits: 'Sync starts with known local runners. nax sync last refreshes the latest local runner. It does not yet discover every remote Agent Runner for a site.' Close that gap:
- nax sync --all: list ALL agent runners for the linked site via the Netlify API (the runners listing endpoint local-runner.js polling already implies), diff against local .nax/agent-runners/, and pull missing/changed ones through the existing syncAgentRunner path (sessions persisted, rollups rebuilt, latest symlinks refreshed)
- nax sync (no arg, TTY): interactive picker of remote runners not yet local — date, agent, status, first-line summary — so the common 'a teammate/another machine ran something' case is a two-keystroke pull
- --since 7d filter to bound API pagination on busy sites; idempotent (re-running syncs nothing new -> says so)

ALSO: GitHub Actions discovery — nax sync --runs lists recent run-nax workflow runs with nax artifacts (gh api filtered by workflow file name) and offers the same pick-and-pull, removing the need to paste run URLs (current github-actions-sync requires explicit URL/run id).

WHY: artifacts are the handoff currency of nax; today they only flow one direction from the machine that orchestrated. Multi-machine and team usage needs pull-based discovery. This also makes 'orchestrate in CI on a schedule, pull results locally' (the schedule bead) a complete loop.

TESTS (extend tests/unit/agent-runner-sync.test.js + github-actions-sync.test.js): discovery diffing (remote superset of local -> exact missing set), --since boundary, idempotent second run, picker payload shape, gh runs listing filter correctness — fixtures captured from real API/gh responses via the transcript recorder.

ACCEPTANCE: delete one local runner dir, run nax sync --all -> exactly that runner restored; second run reports nothing to sync.

title: Visualizer run details modal reuse
description:
WHY: The visualizer currently has two result modals: a rich run-details modal in web/src/components/RecentRuns.tsx and a narrower agent-result modal in web/src/App.tsx. Both ultimately consume the same getRunDetails(runId) endpoint and RunDetailsSection shape, so the duplicated UI/state can be reduced without backend changes.

GOAL: Extract the rich run-details modal into a reusable RunDetailsModal component and route graph agent-result clicks into it when a saved artifact exists. A graph click should deep-link to the selected agent session, keep the full workflow timeline visible, and allow swapping between sibling results. Active live/no-artifact states should keep the current lightweight live modal for the first shipping slice.

CONSTRAINTS:
- No backend/API change expected.
- Preserve RecentRuns behavior during extraction.
- Preserve active live and dry-run UX from App.tsx during Stage 2b.
- Terminal non-completed saved states such as failed, cancelled, abandoned, timeout, and error must be rich-modal eligible.
- Session selection must use exact runnerId/sessionId when available and must not blindly pick the first candidate when multiple sessions exist.
- Verification must include npm run typecheck, npm run visualize:build, and the existing Playwright smoke path where applicable.

SOURCE PLAN: docs/ai/plans/nax-run-details-modal-reuse.md
close_reason: Completed modal reuse refactor, live-context parity, regression coverage, and verification.

title: Extract shared run formatting helpers
description:
STAGE: 0

WHAT: Create web/src/run-format.ts and move the duplicated/near-duplicated formatting helpers out of web/src/App.tsx and web/src/components/RecentRuns.tsx. Export runId, recordValue, recordList, isDoneStatus, agentLabel, statusLabel, statusColor, statusBadgeTone, statusBadgeStyle, statusBadgeColor, and workflowName.

RATIONALE: RunDetailsModal reuse needs App's badge helpers and RecentRuns' run-list helpers without importing component modules from each other. A small shared module keeps the extraction clean and prevents future status-label drift.

IMPORTANT DETAILS:
- Diff the two existing helper copies before merging; do not assume identical behavior.
- Use App.tsx's broader status vocabulary as the canonical superset: waiting, retrying, queued, and error must receive appropriate tones in addition to the RecentRuns states.
- Keep both statusColor and statusBadgeColor exported if call sites remain clearer with both names; they may be thin wrappers over one canonical tone function.
- Update App.tsx and RecentRuns.tsx imports and delete the local duplicates.

ACCEPTANCE:
- npm run typecheck passes.
- Run list badges and existing agent-result badges render with the same intended colors/styles, except for the deliberate broadening for live/error states.
- No modal behavior changes are introduced in this bead beyond helper import paths.
- The diff is limited to helper extraction and import rewiring.
close_reason: Implemented shared run formatting helpers and rewired App/RecentRuns imports.

title: Create pure run-details selection helpers and unit tests
description:
STAGE: 1 prerequisite / selector hardening

WHAT: Extract the session selection logic for RunDetailsModal into a small pure helper that can be reviewed and tested without rendering. The helper should accept RunDetailsSection candidates plus an initial selector { stepId, agent, runnerId?, sessionId? } and return a clear result: exact selected section, no match, or unresolved multiple candidates.

RATIONALE: Stage 2 changes graph agent-result clicks from a narrow App modal to the shared rich modal. The highest-risk behavior is selecting the wrong session when retries/follow-ups produce multiple sections for the same step+agent. Today's App.tsx fallback picks sections[0]; the reusable modal must be stricter.

MATCH CONTRACT:
1. Filter to kind === session, matching stepId and agent.
2. If runnerId or sessionId is supplied, prefer exact runnerId/sessionId match.
3. If exactly one candidate remains, select it.
4. If multiple candidates remain and no exact match exists, do not silently pick the first candidate. Return an unresolved state so UI can show an alert and fall back to summary or leave timeline usable.

TESTS:
- exact runnerId match wins.
- exact sessionId match wins.
- single candidate without exact ids selects successfully.
- multiple candidates without exact id returns unresolved.
- no candidate returns no match.

ACCEPTANCE:
- Tests use the repo's Node test runner style, not a new web component harness.
- npm run typecheck passes.
- The helper is self-contained enough that RunDetailsModal can call it directly in the extraction/rewire beads.
close_reason: Implemented strict run-details selection helper with Node tests.

title: Extract reusable RunDetailsModal component
description:
STAGE: 1

WHAT: Create web/src/components/RunDetailsModal.tsx and move the rich run-details modal currently owned by RecentRuns into it. Use the self-fetching component interface:

{ opened: boolean; onClose: () => void; runId: string; initialSelector?: { stepId: string; agent: string; runnerId?: string; sessionId?: string } }

MOVE INTO COMPONENT:
- detailsLoading, detailsError, detailsResponse, activeTimelineId state.
- getRunDetails(runId) fetch behavior.
- buildStepItems, buildTimelineEntries, stepDescription, timelineBullet, timelineContentTitle, StepItem, TimelineEntry.
- ArtifactActions, RunDetailsMetadata, MetadataRow.
- Existing modal JSX, timeline, content, markdown, metadata, artifact actions, and classNames.

CRITICAL BEHAVIOR:
- Preserve RecentRuns' current default of opening on summary when no initialSelector is supplied.
- Resolve initialSelector to a session entry using the strict selector helper; if unresolved, fall back safely and show an explanatory alert instead of showing the wrong session.
- Add stale-fetch protection with a cancellation flag or request id so a slow prior getRunDetails response cannot overwrite a newer modal open.
- Shared formatting comes from web/src/run-format.ts, not from App.tsx or RecentRuns.tsx.

ACCEPTANCE:
- npm run typecheck passes.
- npm run visualize:build passes.
- Opening run details from the run list remains visually and behaviorally identical: timeline, sibling session navigation, markdown, metadata, ArtifactActions, and Netlify link still work.
- The extraction diff is mostly moves plus the explicit stale-fetch and selector-resolution safeguards.
close_reason: Extracted reusable self-fetching RunDetailsModal with stale fetch protection and selector warnings.

title: Rewire RecentRuns to use RunDetailsModal
description:
STAGE: 1 integration

WHAT: Simplify web/src/components/RecentRuns.tsx so it owns only run-list UI and a selected detailsRunId. Replace the in-file modal state/rendering with <RunDetailsModal opened={Boolean(detailsRunId)} onClose={() => setDetailsRunId('')} runId={detailsRunId} />.

RATIONALE: This is the behavior-preserving call-site conversion after RunDetailsModal exists. It proves the new component can replace the original owner before App graph clicks are rewired.

DETAILS:
- Preserve openRunDetails behavior from the user's perspective: clicking the run title or status/id area opens details for that run.
- RecentRuns should still use shared run-format helpers for run id, status color, and badge style in the list UI.
- Remove moved modal-only helpers/components from RecentRuns after they live in RunDetailsModal.

ACCEPTANCE:
- npm run typecheck passes.
- npm run visualize:build passes.
- npm run visualize:smoke passes or any failure is investigated and documented.
- Manual smoke: run-list details modal opens on summary, timeline navigation works, metadata renders, artifact actions render, and closing/reopening different runs does not show stale content.
close_reason: Rewired RecentRuns to delegate run details rendering to RunDetailsModal.

title: Open RunDetailsModal from graph agent-result clicks
description:
STAGE: 2 / user-visible rewire

WHAT: Update web/src/App.tsx so graph agent-result clicks open the reusable RunDetailsModal for saved artifacts, deep-linked to the clicked agent session. Add App state for detailsModalRunId and detailsModalSelector, render RunDetailsModal near the existing modals, and pass { stepId, agent, runnerId?, sessionId? } as initialSelector.

CRITICAL BEHAVIOR SPLIT:
- Active live/no-artifact states (running, submitted, waiting, retrying, queued) keep the existing lightweight agent-result modal. This preserves live status, runner/session IDs, submitted-after seconds, last-event time, and Netlify links derived from liveRunState.
- Dry-run/no-saved-markdown keeps today's small-modal path that says no dry-run results.
- Saved terminal states (completed, failed, cancelled, abandoned, timeout, error) route to RunDetailsModal.

SESSION METADATA:
- Do not use only completedRunForAgent. Use latest saved run metadata from runForAgent(node, agent) so failed/cancelled/retried artifacts still provide runnerId/sessionId for exact selection.
- Keep selectedRunId || activeRun?.runId as the initial run id source for now, but improve the error copy to say 'Load a saved workflow run before opening agent results' rather than 'completed workflow run'.

RATIONALE: The graph click gate is active && (hasResult || Boolean(agentStatus)), so failed/cancelled/abandoned and active states are intentionally clickable. This bead must preserve those paths while moving saved artifact inspection into the richer modal.

ACCEPTANCE:
- Completed graph agent click opens RunDetailsModal on that agent session and shows sibling timeline navigation.
- Failed/cancelled/abandoned saved agent click opens RunDetailsModal on the exact saved session when runnerId/sessionId exists.
- Active live agent click still opens the existing live panel.
- Dry-run agent click still shows the existing dry-run/no-results path.
- Run-list details path remains unaffected.
- npm run typecheck and npm run visualize:build pass.
close_reason: Graph agent-result clicks now open RunDetailsModal with saved/live context.

title: Add run-details modal regression coverage
description:
STAGE: test coverage for Stages 1 and 2

WHAT: Extend the existing tests around the visualizer refactor. Use the repo's current test stack: Node test runner for pure helpers and Playwright via npm run visualize:smoke for visualizer smoke/E2E.

PURE TESTS:
- Cover the strict session selector helper: exact runnerId, exact sessionId, single candidate, multi-candidate unresolved, and no-match.
- Cover any pure initial-entry/timeline resolution helper introduced during RunDetailsModal extraction, including default-to-summary and selector-to-session resolution.

PLAYWRIGHT / SMOKE:
- Read tests/e2e/visualize.spec.js before editing.
- Keep existing Review graph smoke tests passing.
- Add or extend a scenario that verifies a graph agent-result click opens the rich RunDetailsModal on the correct session when saved details exist.
- Assert sibling timeline navigation still works in the modal.
- Preserve dry-run simulation behavior: dry-run agent click should still show the no-results path, not the rich saved-artifact modal.

VERIFICATION COMMANDS:
- npm run typecheck
- npm run visualize:build
- npm run visualize:smoke

RATIONALE: The refactor is mostly UI movement, but the selector logic has correctness risk and the graph click path is user-visible. Tests should focus there rather than inventing a broad new component-test harness.

ACCEPTANCE:
- New pure tests fail on fallback-first multi-candidate behavior and pass with the stricter unresolved behavior.
- Existing and new Playwright smoke coverage passes.
- Test failures include enough output or locator names to diagnose which modal path regressed.
close_reason: Added selector unit tests and Playwright regression coverage for run-list and graph modal paths.

title: Optional: port live agent panel into RunDetailsModal
description:
OPTIONAL STAGE: 2a after Stage 2b ships

WHAT: Collapse the remaining lightweight agent-result modal path by teaching RunDetailsModal how to render active live/no-artifact agent state. Add an optional live-context prop from App.tsx containing the current active agent status, runner/session IDs, submitted-after seconds, last event time, and live/saved Netlify URL.

RATIONALE: Stage 2b intentionally keeps active live states in the existing modal to reduce risk. Once saved artifacts are handled by RunDetailsModal, this follow-up can make one modal handle all graph agent-result clicks.

EXPECTED BEHAVIOR:
- When the active entry is selected by initialSelector but the section has no markdown yet and live context says the agent is running/submitted/waiting/retrying/queued, render the existing live panel content inside RunDetailsModal's content area.
- Preserve the timeline and metadata layout where possible, but avoid showing misleading 'No result text' for active agents.
- Keep dry-run/no-result messaging explicit.
- After parity is proven, delete the old individual agent-result modal state/rendering from App.tsx.

ACCEPTANCE:
- Active live graph agent click opens RunDetailsModal and shows the same status/runner/session/submitted-after/last-event/link information the old modal showed.
- Completed and terminal saved graph clicks still open the saved markdown session.
- Run-list details path remains unaffected.
- npm run typecheck, npm run visualize:build, and npm run visualize:smoke pass.

NOTE: This is intentionally deferred until after the lower-risk 2b rewire. Do not mix this into the first shipping refactor unless explicitly requested.
close_reason: Ported live agent panel handling into RunDetailsModal via liveContext.

title: Event-driven visualize run status for real-time workflow UI
description:
WHY: The local React Flow visualizer now makes workflow execution understandable, but real runs still get semantic state mostly from coarse polling of .nax/workflows/<run-id>/workflow.json. That is good enough for a prototype and bad enough to miss exactly the states the UI is built to show: step active/done, individual model pills submitting/waiting/completed/failed, retries, cancellation, and artifact availability.

GOAL: Move nax visualize to an event-driven status model. stdout/stderr remain the human terminal stream. Structured runner lifecycle events travel over a dedicated child-process event channel, are persisted to .nax/workflows/<run-id>/events.jsonl, are relayed over SSE to the browser, and are consumed by a typed frontend reducer that drives React Flow cards/model pills without scraping terminal output.

KEY DECISIONS: durable workflow_started handshake is canonical run identity; events.jsonl is v1 scope; remote statuses are best-effort and honest; runner event plumbing is explicit context, not a singleton; retries remain one model pill with attempt metadata; live events win during active runs and durable state wins on load/after exit; the same event log must support future Netlify Functions polling even though hosted orchestration is a separate plan.

REFERENCE PLAN: docs/ai/plans/event-driven-visualize-run-status.md. Beads are intended to be self-contained, but the plan remains the architectural source document.
close_reason: Completed event-driven visualize run status implementation, tests, docs, and packaged UI build.

title: Define runner event protocol, emitter, and append-only event log
description:
WHAT: Add the foundational structured event layer for nax workflow execution.

Implement src/runner-events.js and src/runner-event-log.js (names can change if the codebase suggests better boundaries) with a typed/JSDoc-documented event envelope:
- schemaVersion: 1
- seq: monotonic per durable run id
- eventId: stable dedupe id such as <runId>:<seq>
- type, at, runId, flowId
- optional visualizeRunId, stepId, stepTitle, agent, status, message, data

The emitter must no-op when no event channel is configured so normal nax run behavior is unchanged. When enabled, it writes newline-delimited JSON to fd 3 and appends every structured event to .nax/workflows/<run-id>/events.jsonl. The log writer should be append-only, tolerate process restarts, never include prompt bodies/secrets/large markdown, and expose a reader that can return events after since=<seq>.

WHY: The UI needs semantic lifecycle events, but stdout is human display and workflow.json is a recovery snapshot. events.jsonl becomes the high-fidelity ledger for local SSE, reconnect/debug, and future Netlify Functions polling.

ACCEPTANCE:
- emitter with no NAX_EVENT_FD does nothing and cannot throw
- enabled emitter writes valid one-line JSON events to fd 3
- event log appends ordered seq values and can replay since=N
- malformed input during replay produces a structured parser error rather than crashing
- unit tests cover no-op mode, fd mode, append/replay, seq/eventId, secret/large-payload guardrails
close_reason: Implemented runner event protocol, fd/log emitter, append-only replay, parser diagnostics, and unit tests.

title: Drive React Flow cards, model pills, and edges from live event state
description:
WHAT: Update web/src/components/WorkflowCanvas.tsx, WorkflowNode.tsx, styles, and related App wiring so React Flow visual state comes from the live reducer.

Expected UI behavior:
- step card turns active/yellow when step_status running/waiting arrives
- step card turns completed/green only after step terminal success
- failed/cancelled/abandoned states are visually distinct and not confused with success
- model pills independently show submitting/waiting/active/completed/failed/abandoned
- completed model pill click opens single-agent result details when artifacts exist
- in-progress/abandoned model pill click can show available runner/session metadata or a clear "not available yet" modal
- edges into completed steps become solid done style; edges into in-progress steps stay readable and honest

Preserve current model-selection behavior for non-run definition graphs. Completed/in-flight run graphs should not let a result pill accidentally deselect the model.

WHY: This is the visual payoff of the event work. Users should be able to watch a real run with the same quality as dry-run simulation, without polling lag or weird inferred states.

ACCEPTANCE:
- real event sequence updates cards/pills without fitView/zoom resets from chip clicks
- selected/deselected model affordances remain clear before a run starts
- completed pill opens result modal, dry-run pill still shows no-results message
- visual regressions covered by e2e screenshot or Playwright assertions
close_reason: Live reducer statuses now drive React Flow cards, model pills, edges, and real-result pill behavior without fitView resets.

title: Harden reconnect, replay, and future polling APIs
description:
WHAT: Implement durable replay and reconnect behavior around events.jsonl.

Server/API expectations:
- /api/runs lists active in-memory runs and durable runs
- /api/runs/:id/events replays in-memory events for active local runs
- /api/runs/:runId/events?since=N replays persisted events.jsonl for durable runs
- clients can reconnect after refresh and recover current state from replay + durable graph
- server restart loses child-process ownership but not completed history or event log
- active live events are never overwritten by older durable snapshots

This should intentionally shape the future Netlify Functions polling path even though function-based orchestration is not implemented here.

WHY: SSE is great for local visualize, but durable event logs are what make refresh, debugging, and eventual hosted polling viable.

ACCEPTANCE:
- refresh during active run reconstructs state without waiting for next workflow.json poll
- replay since=N returns only events with seq>N
- server restart still shows completed durable graph/details and can show event history in debug/replay mode
- malformed persisted event lines are reported but do not poison the whole replay
- tests cover replay ordering, dedupe with live stream, and missing/corrupt events.jsonl
close_reason: Added cursor-aware EventSource reconnects, since-aware API URLs, durable malformed-log replay coverage, and verified server replay behavior.

title: Add event diagnostics, raw-event debug view, and user-facing error handling
description:
WHAT: Add observability for the event protocol so failures are debuggable without opening devtools.

UI/server features:
- runner_event_error SSE event when fd 3 or events.jsonl contains malformed event data
- output/status surface for event stream health that does not obscure normal terminal output
- optional raw event debug view or copyable event log panel gated behind a small debug toggle
- clear labels/tooltips for best-effort statuses like waiting and abandoned
- server logs enough context for malformed events without printing secrets or huge payloads

WHY: Event protocols fail in annoying ways: partial chunks, old events, malformed JSON, unknown versions. The first implementation needs diagnostics or every future bug report becomes a mystery.

ACCEPTANCE:
- malformed event line produces visible non-fatal diagnostic
- unknown event type is recorded and ignored for graph state
- debug view can show/copy recent raw structured events
- abandoned/waiting statuses have clear user-facing copy
- tests cover diagnostic event path
close_reason: Added raw event retention, event diagnostics modal, clearer status copy, JSON events diagnostics API, and malformed-event test coverage.

title: Build event-driven visualize integration and e2e test harness
description:
WHAT: Add comprehensive tests proving event-driven visualize works end to end.

Testing layers:
- unit tests for runner-events and runner-event-log
- visualize-server parser/SSE/replay tests using synthetic child fd 3 events
- runner instrumentation tests using injected event sink and real temporary .nax state files
- frontend reducer tests for status mapping and merge precedence
- Playwright e2e using a fake eventing runner mode or test seam to emit workflow_started, step_status, agent_status, artifact_written, stdout, and workflow_completed without spending credits

Scenario assertions:
- step turns active then completed
- model pills complete independently
- output panel streams stdout
- refresh/reconnect recovers state
- since=N replay works
- malformed event is non-fatal and visible

WHY: This feature crosses child process IPC, durable files, HTTP/SSE, React reducer state, and React Flow rendering. It needs tests at each boundary or regressions will be hard to localize.

ACCEPTANCE:
- no real Netlify credits required in CI
- tests use real filesystem/event log behavior, not mocked logic where a real temp file can be used
- Playwright assertions cover visible card/pill state transitions
- failure logs include event sequence and reducer state for diagnosis
close_reason: Added no-credit Playwright dry-run transition coverage, fixed production TDZ regression, rebuilt visualizer, and verified unit/integration/e2e event paths.

title: Document event-driven visualize architecture and developer workflows
description:
WHAT: Update docs after implementation so future maintainers understand the event architecture.

Documentation should cover:
- stdout/stderr vs fd 3 structured events
- event envelope and event types
- events.jsonl location and replay semantics
- durable workflow.json relationship
- status vocabulary and best-effort remote status caveats
- cancellation vs abandoned remote work
- local SSE path vs future polling-friendly event log
- how to run the event-driven visualize tests
- troubleshooting malformed event stream diagnostics

Likely locations: docs/ai/plans/event-driven-visualize-run-status.md as implemented notes, README if user-facing, and/or an internal docs/ai architecture note.

WHY: The event stream becomes a real internal API. Without docs, future runner or UI work will accidentally regress identity, replay, or status semantics.

ACCEPTANCE:
- docs explain how UI receives updates from the running process
- docs explicitly say no stdout parsing for semantics
- docs show where to find events.jsonl and how since replay works
- docs note Netlify Functions orchestration is future separate work that can reuse the event log
close_reason: Documented event-driven visualize architecture, replay endpoints, diagnostics, caveats, and validation workflow.

title: Wire visualize server to parse fd 3 runner events and relay SSE
description:
WHAT: Update src/visualize-server.js runWorkflowChild/startRun handling so child nax processes are spawned with stdio [ignore, pipe, pipe, pipe], NAX_EVENT_FD=3, and any needed NAX_EVENT_STREAM=jsonl env.

Add a robust fd 3 JSONL parser that handles partial chunks, multiple events in one chunk, trailing incomplete data, malformed lines, unknown event types, and child close while a partial line remains. Parsed runner events should be recorded into the existing in-memory run.events array and relayed to connected EventSource clients using the event type as the SSE event name.

Keep existing stdout/stderr SSE untouched. stdout/stderr are still appended to the output panel and still useful for users. Structured runner events must not be inferred from stdout.

Add /api/runs/:runId/events?since=<seq> replay for persisted durable event logs. Existing active visualize-run event streams should still replay in-memory events. When a durable run id is known, the server should be able to combine/replay persisted events for reconnect/debug without relying on process memory alone.

WHY: This is the bridge between the runner event protocol and the React UI. The server must be resilient because one bad event line must never kill the local visualizer.

ACCEPTANCE:
- synthetic child fd 3 events arrive as SSE events in order
- stdout/stderr streaming behavior is unchanged
- invalid JSONL emits runner_event_error and continues
- active runs can replay in-memory events
- durable runs can replay events.jsonl with since=N
- tests cover parser chunking, malformed events, unknown types, and closed child behavior
close_reason: Spawn visualize child with fd 3, parse JSONL runner events, relay SSE, and replay durable events.jsonl with tests.

title: Make workflow_started the durable run identity handshake
description:
WHAT: Stop depending on stdout text like "Run ..." or "State: .../workflow.json" as the primary way the visualizer discovers the durable run id.

After handleRun creates and saves the run state, emit workflow_started immediately with runId, flowId, flowTitle, projectRoot, transport, branch, command/options metadata that is safe to expose, and status=running. The visualize server should bind its temporary local visualize run id to this durable run id when the event arrives. The UI may start with the temporary id returned by POST /runs, but all graph/details/history identity should switch to the durable id as soon as the handshake is received.

Keep extractDurableRunId as fallback during transition only. It should no longer be the happy path.

WHY: Run identity is currently fragile because the server scrapes stdout. Event-driven UI needs a canonical id before step/agent events can safely map to graph nodes, artifact paths, and event logs.

ACCEPTANCE:
- workflow_started is emitted before the first step submission
- visualize server public run object gains runId without parsing stdout
- /api/runs/:id/graph and /details work with either temporary visualize id or durable run id during transition
- refresh during active run can recover durable identity from event log or server memory
- tests prove stdout without State/Run lines still produces a usable run identity
close_reason: workflow_started is emitted from durable run state through explicit runtime events; visualize server binds runId from runner events with stdout scraping only as fallback.

title: Thread explicit runtime event context through workflow execution
description:
WHAT: Introduce an explicit runtime/context object that carries the event emitter through workflow execution. Thread it through handleRun -> executeLocalFlow/executeGithubFlow -> completeLocalStep/completeGithubStep -> waitForLocalAgentRuns/waitForGithubStep callback boundaries, without relying on module-level singletons.

This context should also be usable by in-process runWorkflow() for dry-run/server tests. It should expose small methods like emitWorkflowStatus, emitStepStatus, emitAgentStatus, emitArtifactWritten, or a generic emit with helper wrappers. Keep the public function surfaces conservative and consistent with repo style.

WHY: Explicit plumbing is more work now but avoids hidden global state, makes tests deterministic, allows future Netlify Functions orchestration to create isolated runtimes, and avoids accidental cross-talk if two runs execute in one Node process later.

ACCEPTANCE:
- normal CLI execution without an event context is unchanged
- tests can inject a fake event sink into the runner without spawning a child process
- no module-level global event bus is required for normal workflow execution
- existing unit tests continue to pass with minimal API churn
- code comments explain the context boundary only where it prevents confusion
close_reason: Added explicit workflow event context and threaded it through primary local/GitHub execution paths with injectable sink support.

title: Emit Netlify API step and agent lifecycle events
description:
WHAT: Instrument the Netlify API/local-machine workflow path in bin/nax.js and src/local-runner.js so real runs emit semantic events for every meaningful step and agent transition.

Expected event flow:
- step_status running when a step starts and is persisted to workflow.json
- agent_status pending for selected agents when stepState.runs is initialized
- agent_status submitting before submitLocalAgentRun starts
- agent_status submitted when Netlify returns runnerId/sessionId/links
- agent_status waiting while Nax polls remote status and cannot prove live model execution
- agent_status running only if Netlify status data actually proves active execution
- agent_status completed/failed/timeout when waitForLocalAgentRuns/onTerminalRun produces terminal result
- step_status completed/failed/cancelled when localStepStatus resolves

Retries and compact-prompt retries should stay attached to the same model pill with attempt, attemptId, previousRunnerId, retry reason, and new runner/session ids in event metadata.

WHY: This is the core UX win. The dry-run simulation already feels right because it owns per-agent timings. Real Netlify API runs need equivalent honest events from the actual execution path.

ACCEPTANCE:
- deselecting models/stepModels still results in only selected agents receiving active events
- first Netlify step in a real run lights step card and per-agent pills without waiting for workflow.json polling
- submitted events include runner/session ids and links when available
- terminal events include usage/error/result metadata references without embedding large result text
- retry attempts update one pill and preserve attempt metadata for details
- tests cover submission success, submission failure, waiting, terminal success, terminal failure, and retry metadata
close_reason: Instrumented Netlify API path with step_status and agent_status events for pending/submitting/submitted/waiting/running/terminal/retry states.

title: Emit GitHub transport lifecycle events with honest best-effort semantics
description:
WHAT: Instrument the GitHub Actions transport path so it emits the same event shapes as Netlify API where the information exists, while remaining honest about states GitHub cannot prove.

Expected behavior:
- step_status running when a GitHub-backed step starts
- agent_status submitting/submitted when issues/comments/workflow dispatches are created
- agent_status waiting while polling GitHub comments/action runs
- agent_status completed when a result comment/reply is found and normalized
- agent_status failed/timeout for action launch failures, runner failure markers, or poll timeout
- step_status completed/failed according to existing githubStepStatus semantics

Do not fake detailed remote-running states. If GitHub only proves that work was submitted and Nax is polling, use waiting.

WHY: The UI should not be Netlify-API-only. The same reducer and graph rendering should work across transports, even if GitHub provides fewer lifecycle details.

ACCEPTANCE:
- GitHub happy path emits step and per-agent terminal events
- action launch failure emits failed event with actionRunUrl/failureKind when available
- timeout emits failed visual state while preserving raw timeout status metadata
- existing GitHub behavior and artifact shape remain unchanged
- tests cover comment result success, runner failure marker, action launch failure, and timeout
close_reason: Instrumented GitHub transport with best-effort step/agent lifecycle events and explicit submission failure events.

title: Emit artifact_written events and define durable milestone write policy
description:
WHAT: Add artifact_written events around workflow artifact persistence without turning events into bulky content transport.

Instrument workflow-artifacts paths so the runner emits artifact_written for workflow summary, step summary, agent result markdown, agent metadata JSON, usage, and final-result artifacts. Events should include runId, flowId, stepId when relevant, agent when relevant, artifactType, run-relative path, and absolute path for local visualizer open/copy actions.

Also codify and enforce the durable write policy: events.jsonl gets every structured lifecycle event; workflow.json remains milestone state. workflow.json should be saved after run creation, step start, run initialization, submission ids, terminal agent results, step terminal state, and workflow terminal state. Do not save workflow.json for animation-only transitions.

WHY: The UI needs to know when result modals can load or refresh, but large markdown/results belong in artifacts. Keeping workflow.json as a snapshot avoids bloating the recovery file with transient state.

ACCEPTANCE:
- artifact_written events fire for step and agent artifacts after files exist
- event payload contains both relative and absolute paths where applicable
- no prompt bodies or large result markdown are embedded in events
- workflow.json remains compact and valid
- tests verify artifact events and milestone write timing on a small fixture run
close_reason: Added artifact_written events after workflow, step, and agent artifact files are persisted while keeping payloads path-only.

title: Implement local cancellation and abandoned remote-agent event semantics
description:
WHAT: Make cancellation semantics explicit in the event stream and UI state model.

When the user cancels from visualize, the server already sends SIGTERM/SIGKILL to the local child. Extend the runner/server event handling so cancellation emits workflow_status or workflow_completed cancelled, step_status cancelled for active local orchestration, and agent_status abandoned for remote jobs that may continue outside Nax observation. Only emit agent_status cancelled when Nax truly cancelled before remote submission or can prove remote cancellation happened.

Do not implement remote Netlify/GitHub job cancellation/archive in this bead. That is intentionally separate product/API work.

WHY: Users need honest state. If remote agent runs keep executing after local orchestration stops, the UI must not imply they were actually cancelled.

ACCEPTANCE:
