Separately, nax's primary emerging audience is AI agents driving it from other sessions (handoff is built for exactly this), but output is human boxes/spinners only — agents must scrape pretty-printed text.

GOAL: One command that proves the environment is ready, and machine-readable output everywhere an agent would consume results.

PARTS: nax doctor (diagnose + actionable fixes), --json on read commands (list, recent, handoff sources, usage, run completion summary), and non-TTY sanity (no spinner garbage when piped).

SERVES: intuitive/ergonomic pillar; turns the troubleshooting table from documentation into executable checks; makes nax composable in scripts and agent harnesses.

title: nax doctor: executable environment diagnostics
description:
WHAT: New command nax doctor running ordered checks, each -> pass/warn/fail + one-line actionable fix:
1. node version >= engines.node
2. gh installed + authenticated (gh auth status) + repo detected
3. netlify CLI installed + authenticated + linked site (.netlify/state.json or NETLIFY_SITE_ID)
4. github-actions transport readiness: .github/workflows agent-runner workflow present (reuse src/init.js findExistingAgentRunnerWorkflow), repo secrets NETLIFY_SITE_ID/NETLIFY_AUTH_TOKEN present (gh secret list)
5. netlify-api transport readiness: API reachable, site id resolvable
6. flows: bundled flows load, project flowsDirs exist, every flow passes validation (reuses flow lint bead when it lands; until then basic load check)
7. .nax health: symlink support, no stale run.lock, unfinished workflows count

OUTPUT: box-logger table for humans; --json emits { checks: [{ id, status, detail, fix }] , ok: bool }; exit code 0 only if no fails (warns ok) so CI can gate on it.

IMPLEMENTATION: src/doctor.js with each check a small async fn { id, title, run() } — registry pattern so future beads (e.g. agent registry) append checks. Reuse existing detection code in transports.js/init.js — do not duplicate it.

ACCEPTANCE: on a fresh unwired repo, doctor lists exactly what init would fix and names init as the fix; on a wired repo all green.

title: Tests: doctor checks + json contract snapshots
description:
WHAT: tests/unit/doctor.test.js + tests/unit/json-output.test.js.

DOCTOR: each check tested against real fixture dirs in tmp (e.g. repo without .github workflow -> check 4 fails with fix text mentioning nax init); checks that shell out (gh auth status) take an injectable exec seam like existing gh-cli tests; exit code semantics (fail -> 1, warn-only -> 0).

JSON: golden-shape assertions for list/recent/handoff/run-summary payloads — assert required keys + types, not exact values, so additive evolution is allowed but breakage is caught. Assert stdout/stderr separation by capturing both streams. Assert non-TTY mode (isTTY false) produces zero ANSI escape sequences on stdout (regex scan).

LOGGING: every assertion failure prints the full captured payload for diagnosis.

title: JSON robot mode (--json) for read commands + non-TTY output sanity
description:
WHAT: Add --json to: nax list (flows with id/title/description/source/steps), nax recent (artifact entries as handoff-sources already models them), nax handoff --json (print selected source as { type, id, path, summary } instead of interactive), and end-of-run summary (nax run ... --json prints final machine summary: runId, status, per-step per-agent statuses, artifact paths, usage rollup) while human output still goes to stderr in that mode.

CONTRACT: JSON goes to stdout ONLY; all decorative output to stderr when --json is set. Stable keys documented in README. This is the integration surface for other agents driving nax — treat key naming as API (camelCase, match workflow.json field names where they already exist).

NON-TTY: when stdout is not a TTY (piped), suppress spinners/interactive pickers; commands that would prompt should fail with a clear 'missing --flag for non-interactive use' message naming the flag. Audit existing process.stdout.isTTY usage for consistency.

ACCEPTANCE: nax list --json | jq works; nax review --force --json in a pipe emits exactly one JSON doc on stdout; no ANSI codes inside JSON.

title: Flow authoring toolkit: nax lint + nax flow new + fail-fast validation
description:
WHY: Custom flows are the extensibility story ('the flow file is the program') but authoring one today is copy-a-bundled-flow-and-pray. Errors (typo'd step id in input.step, missing prompt file, unknown agent name, invalid action/submit combo) surface mid-run — possibly after credits are spent on earlier steps. There is no schema document, no validator, no scaffolder.

GOAL: writing a flow is guided, and a broken flow can never start executing.

PIECES: nax lint <flow> (full static validation + plan render), automatic pre-run validation (same code path, fail before any submission), nax flow new <id> scaffolder.

SERVES: this epic is the foundation other flow-feature epics build on — control-flow keys (onFailure/when/inputs) and the pluggable agent registry both extend the schema this epic formalizes. Sequence it first among flow work.

title: Flow schema + nax lint static validation
description:
WHAT: src/flow-schema.js — single source of truth for flow shape (JSDoc typedefs + a validate(flow, { flowDir }) returning [{ level: error|warn, path, message, fix }]).

CHECKS (each with actionable message):
- required: id, steps[].id unique, steps[].prompt
- prompt files exist relative to flow dir; warn on unused files in prompts/
- enum fields: action (issue|comment), submit (new-run|follow-up), waitFor, agents subset of known registry
- semantic: input[].step references an EARLIER step id (forward/self refs are errors -> cycle-impossible by construction); submit:follow-up requires an input step that produced a runner; action:comment requires an input source; agents non-empty after defaults merge
- defaults merge correctness (step overrides flow defaults)
- warn: step count 1 with waitFor set (pointless), missing title/description (picker UX degrades)

COMMAND: nax lint [flow] — all flows when omitted; renders the same plan boxes as preview-boxes on success; --json list of diagnostics; exit 1 on any error-level finding.

NOTE: validation must load flows through the existing configorama path so JS/TS/TOML/JSON flows are validated post-evaluation, same as runtime sees them.

ACCEPTANCE: seeding each defect above into a fixture flow yields exactly one targeted diagnostic naming the path (steps[2].input[0].step) and a fix hint.

title: Pre-run fail-fast: validate every flow before any submission
description:
WHAT: handleRun calls flow-schema validate immediately after flow load, before transport detection, context building, or ANY issue/runner creation. Error-level findings abort with the lint-style report ('flow review-custom failed validation, fix or run nax lint review-custom'). Warn-level findings print but proceed. --dry also runs validation (so dry-run is a real preflight).

Also validate at picker time: flows failing validation appear in the picker greyed/flagged '(invalid: 2 errors)' rather than hidden — discoverable but not runnable.

WHY SEPARATE BEAD: wiring is small but behavioral surface (picker, dry-run, run) needs its own tests and could regress UX if rushed in with the validator itself.

ACCEPTANCE: nax run against a flow with a missing prompt file exits before creating anything, with the same diagnostic nax lint shows; credits spent = 0.

title: nax flow new: interactive flow scaffolder
description:
WHAT: nax flow new <id> generates a project-local flow under the first configured flowsDir (default .github/nax-flows/<id>/).

INTERACTIVE (clack): title, description, number of steps, per step: title, action, submit, agents (multiselect), whether it consumes prior results. Non-interactive: --steps 3 --agents claude,codex --yes produces a sensible review-shaped template.

OUTPUT: flow.yml (with comments explaining each key inline — the generated file doubles as schema documentation) + prompts/N_<step-id>.md stubs each containing a heading, a TODO marker, and the structured-output instruction block matching the findings.json contract so custom flows are findings-compatible by default.

GUARANTEE: generated flow passes nax lint with zero errors/warnings (enforced in tests). If <id> collides with bundled flow, warn that project flow will shadow it (that is the documented precedence) and require --force.

ACCEPTANCE: nax flow new conversion-audit --steps 2 --yes && nax lint conversion-audit -> clean; flow appears in picker under project workflows.

title: Tests: schema validator matrix, fail-fast wiring, scaffold round-trip
description:
WHAT: tests/unit/flow-schema.test.js + flow-new.test.js + scenarios in flows.test.js.

VALIDATOR MATRIX: one fixture per defect class (12+ cases from the lint bead list); assert diagnostic count, level, path string, fix hint presence. Valid bundled flows: assert ALL 14 bundled flows pass with zero errors (this doubles as a regression net for bundled flow edits — a failing bundled flow should fail CI).

FAIL-FAST: run path with invalid flow -> no gh/runner calls attempted (assert via exec seam call log), exit code 1, stderr contains diagnostic; warn-only flow proceeds; picker shows invalid marker.

SCAFFOLD: generate in tmp across formats default, then lint -> zero diagnostics; round-trip load through configorama equals expected structure; collision without --force refused.

LOGGING: diagnostics printed verbatim on failure.

title: Nax Visualize: React Flow workflow UI and local operator cockpit
description:
WHY: Nax workflows are currently discoverable and previewable in the terminal, but the mental model is graph-shaped: steps fan out to agents, gate on results, feed later steps, and produce durable artifacts. A local UI can make that workflow structure inspectable at a glance and eventually become the safest operator surface for dry-runs, real runs, live status, and artifact handoff.

GOAL: Add `nax visualize` as a local browser workbench for workflow discovery, React Flow graph rendering, dry-run preview, run submission, live status, and artifact browsing. The first shippable increment must be read-only and low-risk: list workflows from the same loader as the CLI and render selected workflows as React Flow nodes/edges. Later increments add privileged run control through a localhost API with explicit safety boundaries.

CONTEXT: Existing source of truth is `src/flows.js` (`listFlows`, `loadFlow`, `loadStepPrompt`). The terminal preview lives in `bin/nax.js` via `printFlowPlan`. Actual orchestration is currently entangled in `handleRun()` inside `bin/nax.js`, so supported browser-triggered runs eventually need orchestration extracted into reusable modules. The package is CommonJS and currently advertises Node >=18, while Vite 8 tooling requires Node 20.19+ or 22.12+; end users should not need Vite at runtime if built assets ship in npm.

NON-NEGOTIABLES: The browser never calls Netlify or GitHub directly. The visualize server binds to localhost by default, validates workflow ids and options, uses argv arrays instead of shell strings for delegated commands, and protects mutating endpoints with a per-process token. The UI should be a dense operational workbench, not a landing page.

PHASING: Phase 1 read-only visualizer; Phase 2 dry-run/command preview; Phase 3 real run submission with streamed events; Phase 4 reusable orchestration extraction; Phase 5 artifact/run browser.
close_reason: Completed Nax Visualize React Flow UI graph: workflow discovery, React Flow rendering, dry-run preview, safe run control, artifact/run browsing, direct runner extraction, tests, docs, and validation.

title: Resolve visualize product and safety defaults before run-control work
description:
WHAT: Resolve the open product questions before implementing run-control defaults: whether `nax visualize` help text is marked experimental, whether the first run button is dry-run only, whether real runs require an extra flag or explicit UI confirmation, whether the UI should auto-open latest unfinished workflow, whether workflow editing is a future goal or explicitly out of scope, and whether branch selection defaults to existing `resolveWorkflowBranch()` logic.

WHY: These choices shape safety, UX, and implementation boundaries. Deciding them in plan space avoids a UI that accidentally normalizes expensive remote actions.

ACCEPTANCE: Decisions are recorded in bead notes or implementation docs and reflected in command help/UI copy. Real-run implementation beads can proceed without ambiguity about default behavior.
notes: Decisions for implementation: mark `nax visualize` experimental for the current release; dry-run remains the primary visible action; real runs are allowed only after an explicit browser confirmation that shows workflow, project root, branch, transport, selected models, and remote side effects; all mutating endpoints require the per-process visualize token; workflow editing is out of scope for this graph; branch defaults remain a simple UI value for now, with CLI-side `resolveWorkflowBranch()` still authoritative when a run is invoked.
close_reason: Recorded safety/product defaults for visualize run-control: experimental command, dry-run primary, confirmed real runs, token-protected mutations, no workflow editing, CLI branch resolution remains authoritative.

title: Document full visualize UI, run-control, and artifact browser
description:
WHAT: Document the full visualize surface after dry-run, real-run, and artifact browser phases land. Update README command sections, examples, safety notes, troubleshooting, and any docs/ai references. Include `nax visualize`, dry-run from UI, real-run confirmation, token-protected localhost API behavior at a high level, artifact browser, and Node/Vite version expectations for development.

WHY: Once the UI can create remote side effects, stale or underspecified docs become a safety problem. Users need to understand what runs locally, what talks to Netlify/GitHub, and how to recover artifacts.

ACCEPTANCE: README/docs match shipped behavior for read-only, dry-run, real-run, and artifact browser surfaces. Troubleshooting includes missing built assets, port conflicts, bad token, invalid workflow, and missing Netlify/GitHub prerequisites. Existing docs wave can reference or absorb this bead if desired.
close_reason: Documented visualize workflow list, React Flow graph, dry-run, run submission, recent artifacts, token behavior, Vite tooling, and troubleshooting.

title: Dashboard route consolidation: TanStack Router as source of truth
description:
WHY: The dashboard currently has URL-addressable routes, but route ownership is split between TanStack Router declarations in src/dashboard/web/src/router.tsx and a manual pathname parser in src/dashboard/web/src/dashboard-routes.ts. App.tsx reads useRouterState().location.pathname, calls parseDashboardPath(), and then derives selected workflow/run/step/modal state from that separate parser. This makes every route addition or change easy to desynchronize: a path can exist in the router but parse differently, or helper builders can drift from route declarations.

GOAL: Make TanStack Router the single source of truth for dashboard view state, route params, modal destinations, and navigation helpers while keeping existing URL shapes stable. The dashboard must continue to support workflow configure routes, prompt routes, run inspect routes, run summary routes, step routes, and agent-specific run details routes.

NON-GOALS: Do not redesign the visual dashboard layout. Do not replace TanStack Query server-state hooks. Do not introduce file-based routing. Do not change URL paths unless a bug requires it. Do not solve hosted auth/API-base concerns here.

SOURCE PLAN: docs/ai/plans/nax-dashboard-route-consolidation.md

ACCEPTANCE: App.tsx no longer calls parseDashboardPath; route declarations and path builders share one route spec; existing dashboard URLs still work; prompt and run-details modal routes still deep-link; npm run typecheck, npm test or focused route tests, and npm run dashboard:build pass.
close_reason: Dashboard route consolidation completed: TanStack route spec drives router declarations, path builders, route state, navigation, deep-link smoke tests, and docs.

title: Route regression matrix and router test harness
description:
Create a route regression test harness and explicit route matrix before changing production routing. The harness should encode all dashboard URL shapes and user-facing semantics so later implementation beads can move behavior from parseDashboardPath to TanStack Router without losing deep-link behavior.

Scope:
- Cover /, /workflows, /workflows/$workflowId, /workflows/$workflowId/steps/$stepId, /workflows/$workflowId/prompts/$stepId, /runs, /runs/$runId, /runs/$runId/details, /runs/$runId/steps/$stepId, and /runs/$runId/steps/$stepId/agents/$agent.
- Include encoded id cases: spaces, punctuation, and values containing encoded slash (%2F) where the existing route behavior permits it.
- Assert route intent, not just string equality: configure vs inspect mode, workflow id, run id, step id, prompt-modal state, run-details-modal state, and agent id.
- Preserve legacy ?workflow= behavior until a separate removal decision is made.
- Avoid reimplementing a new parser in tests; the end-state tests should instantiate or exercise router matching/adapter output.

Rationale: This is the safety net for a large App.tsx routing change. The first implementation may temporarily compare current parseDashboardPath output to the future match adapter, but final tests should validate router-driven behavior.

Acceptance:
- A focused unit test file documents the full route matrix.
- Tests fail if route declarations, path builders, and route-state interpretation drift.
- Existing dashboard-query-cache route parser tests still pass until the manual parser is removed.
- Command: node --import tsx --test <focused route test file> passes.
close_reason: Route regression matrix and router test harness implemented in tests/unit/dashboard-routes.test.ts; covers declared route paths, encoded params, legacy query behavior, and router-derived route state.

title: Introduce shared dashboard route spec and path builders
description:
Introduce a shared route specification module that becomes the source for both TanStack route declarations and URL path builders.

Current problem: router.tsx declares paths, while dashboard-routes.ts separately defines path builders and parseDashboardPath. The route spec should remove duplicated route literals as the first behavior-preserving step.

Implementation outline:
- Add src/dashboard/web/src/route-spec.ts or equivalent.
- Define stable route ids and route path templates for all current dashboard routes.
- Move or re-export URL builders such as workflowPath, workflowStepPath, workflowPromptPath, runPath, runDetailsPath, runStepPath, and runAgentPath from the route spec.
- Keep URL encoding centralized in builders.
- Update router.tsx to import path templates/spec entries rather than carrying independent string literals.
- Keep parseDashboardPath in place for this bead so production behavior remains unchanged.

Design constraints:
- Do not change existing URL shapes.
- Do not change App.tsx route consumption yet.
- Keep helpers ergonomic for callers; callers should not manually concatenate route strings.

Acceptance:
- router.tsx and path builders share the route spec.
- Existing route parser tests still pass.
- New tests prove every builder maps to an existing route declaration.
- npm run typecheck and npm run dashboard:build pass.
close_reason: Shared dashboard route spec and path builders added in src/dashboard/web/src/route-spec.ts; router declarations and App navigation consume the shared spec/builders.

title: Add match-based dashboard route-state adapter
description:
Add a match-based route-state adapter that derives DashboardRouteState from TanStack Router matches/params instead of parsing location.pathname.

Current production state uses parseDashboardPath(pathname). This bead creates the bridge that lets App.tsx switch safely without rewriting every render branch at once.

Implementation outline:
- Add routeStateFromMatches or routeStateFromRouterState using TanStack Router match data.
- Preserve the existing DashboardRouteState union shape initially so downstream selectors routeWorkflowId, routeRunId, routeWorkflowStepId, routeRunStepId, routePromptStepId, etc. can continue to work.
- Use route ids and params from the route spec/router, not regexes over pathname.
- Add tests comparing match-based route state to the existing parser for the known route matrix during migration.
- Include prompt route and run detail modal route cases explicitly.

Important edge cases:
- Unknown routes should fall back intentionally, matching current home/not-found behavior.
- Encoded params must be handled consistently with current links.
- Browser refresh/deep-link semantics must be preserved because users rely on local URLs to return to a run or step.

Acceptance:
- A match-based adapter exists and is tested.
- Adapter tests cover every current route shape.
- App.tsx is not switched yet, unless the change is trivially safe and still fully covered.
- npm run typecheck and focused route tests pass.
close_reason: Match-based dashboard route-state adapter added as dashboardRouteStateFromMatches, deriving selected workflow/run/modal state from TanStack Router matches.

title: Switch App.tsx to router-match route state
description:
Switch App.tsx from manual pathname parsing to the match-based route adapter. This is the production behavior change that removes the most important duplication.

Current code pattern to remove:
const pathname = useRouterState({ select: (state) => state.location.pathname })
const routeState = useMemo(() => parseDashboardPath(pathname), [pathname])

Implementation outline:
- Use TanStack Router state/matches and the adapter from the prior bead.
- Preserve existing routeState consumers where that keeps the change smaller.
- Verify workflow configure routes still render configure mode and use Inspector for step clicks.
- Verify run inspect routes still render inspect mode and open RunDetailsModal for step/agent routes.
- Verify WorkflowPromptModal still opens from prompt routes and updates URL when switching prompt steps.
- Verify modal close behavior routes users back to the correct parent path.

Risk areas:
- App.tsx is large and route state influences selected graph, details modal context, prompt modal state, and navigation helpers.
- Avoid opportunistic UI refactors in this bead. The goal is route source-of-truth, not component decomposition.

Acceptance:
- App.tsx no longer calls parseDashboardPath.
- Existing route-driven workflows behave the same.
- Route-state adapter unit tests pass.
- npm run typecheck and npm run dashboard:build pass.
close_reason: App.tsx now selects route state from router matches via useRouterState and shared route specs instead of parsing location.pathname manually.

title: Delete legacy parseDashboardPath parser and parser tests
description:
Remove the legacy manual pathname parser once App.tsx no longer depends on it.

Scope:
- Delete parseDashboardPath if it has no production consumers.
- Remove or replace tests that assert manual regex/pathname parsing directly.
- Keep useful selector helpers only if they operate on router-derived route state and do not parse strings.
- Run rg parseDashboardPath and ensure no production usage remains.
- Update imports across App.tsx, tests, and helper modules.

Rationale: Leaving the old parser in the codebase invites future features to depend on it again, recreating the dual-source-of-truth problem this work is meant to solve.

Acceptance:
- rg "parseDashboardPath" finds no production usage; ideally no usage at all.
- Route tests validate router matching/path building instead of parser behavior.
- npm run check, npm test or focused route tests, and npm run dashboard:build pass.
close_reason: Legacy parseDashboardPath parser and parser-focused tests removed; no source or test imports remain, with historical references kept only in the plan docs.

title: Add dashboard deep-link and modal route smoke coverage
description:
Add or update dashboard route e2e/smoke coverage for the user workflows that route consolidation must preserve.

Scope:
- Refresh/deep-link to /workflows/<workflowId> and verify configure mode appears.
- Deep-link to /workflows/<workflowId>/steps/<stepId> and verify the step inspector reflects that step.
- Deep-link to /workflows/<workflowId>/prompts/<stepId> and verify WorkflowPromptModal opens to the right prompt.
- Deep-link to /runs/<runId>/details, /runs/<runId>/steps/<stepId>, and /runs/<runId>/steps/<stepId>/agents/<agent> using a fixture run, and verify RunDetailsModal opens correctly.
- Exercise browser back/forward for at least one prompt route and one run-details route.
- Keep logging detailed enough that failures show the attempted URL and expected route mode/modal target.

Implementation notes:
- Prefer extending existing Playwright dashboard smoke fixtures if practical.
- Do not make the test dependent on live remote services.
- Use durable fixture data under a temporary project root.

Acceptance:
- E2E/smoke route tests pass locally.
- npm run dashboard:smoke passes if the suite remains cheap and reliable.
- npm run dashboard:build passes.
close_reason: Playwright dashboard smoke coverage now exercises workflow, prompt modal, run details, run step, and run agent deep links with browser history behavior.

title: Optional route-aware App.tsx component boundaries
description:
After route-state ownership is fixed, consider extracting route-aware view boundaries from App.tsx only if it improves clarity without changing behavior.

Possible components:
- WorkflowRouteView: workflow configure graph, inspector route state, prompt route bridge.
- RunRouteView: run graph inspect mode, run details route bridge, selected run state.
- PromptRouteModalBridge: opens/closes WorkflowPromptModal based on route state.
- RunDetailsRouteModalBridge: opens/closes RunDetailsModal based on route state.

Constraints:
- This is cleanup after the functional migration, not a prerequisite for removing parseDashboardPath.
- Do not introduce card-inside-card UI changes or visual redesign.
- Keep shared shell concerns in App.tsx: navbar, theme, splitter sizing, dry-run context modal, event diagnostics, and global error state unless extraction is obviously safe.

Acceptance:
- If extraction is done, it is behavior-preserving and covered by route unit/e2e tests.
- If extraction is skipped, leave a short note in the implementation PR explaining that App.tsx remained readable enough after the routing migration.
- npm run typecheck and npm run dashboard:build pass.
close_reason: Optional App.tsx extraction was evaluated and intentionally skipped; after parser removal, route-facing code remains localized and readable, and the plan documents the decision.

title: Document dashboard route source-of-truth after migration
description:
Update project documentation and implementation notes after route consolidation lands.

Scope:
- Update docs/ai/plans/nax-dashboard-route-consolidation.md status/implementation notes if the plan is kept as a living document.
- If README or dashboard docs mention route behavior, make sure examples use the current URL shapes.
- Document the route source-of-truth module and how to add a new dashboard route without reintroducing a parallel parser.
- Include validation commands that were run.

Acceptance:
- Future contributors can find the route spec and understand the rule: routes and path builders come from one source.
- No stale mention of parseDashboardPath as the production route interpreter remains in docs.
- Documentation changes are concise and do not duplicate implementation details unnecessarily.
close_reason: Dashboard route source-of-truth docs updated to describe route-spec.ts, router-match state, implemented tests, and the optional extraction decision.

title: Documentation wave: README + docs for all new surface area
description:
WHAT: The README is the product (35KB, carefully structured: TL;DR, comparison table, flow anatomy, troubleshooting). Every epic in this graph adds user-facing surface that must land there with the same quality bar, and stale docs are worse than no docs.

SCOPE per shipped epic/feature (check off as each lands — this bead stays open as the docs gate until the graph completes):
- Commands table: doctor, usage, clean, watch, schedule, flow new, lint, handoff --to targets
- Flags table: --json, --base, --var, --max-credits, --notify-url, --retry-attempts, --no-lock
- Flow Anatomy: inputs:, when:, onFailure:/maxRetries: keys with the same key-table treatment as existing step keys; flow-schema as the authoritative reference
- New sections: 'Cost' (usage + budgets + estimates), 'Findings pipeline' (findings.json schema + handoff targets diagram), 'Scheduled flows'
- Troubleshooting table: rows for every new failure mode introduced (lock refusal, budget abort, validation failure, webhook delivery warning) — each row maps to a doctor check where applicable
- Limitations section: REMOVE lines this graph fixes (three-agents hardcoded, macOS-only notifications, sync discovery) as they ship — stale limitations erode trust in the whole list
- docs/ai/: findings.json schema doc, transcript recording how-to, flow-schema reference generated or hand-written

QUALITY BAR: match existing README voice (terse, table-driven, no marketing fluff); every code fence language-tagged per repo markdown rules.

title: Webhook notifications: --notify-url + failure notices
description:
WHAT: Generalize notifications beyond macOS osascript:
- --notify-url <url> (+ nax.config.json notifyUrl, env NAX_NOTIFY_URL): POST JSON { event: workflow-completed|workflow-failed|workflow-interrupted|step-completed, runId, flowId, flowTitle, branch, status, durationMs, usage: { totalCredits }, summaryPath, links } on terminal events (step-completed only with --notify-steps, off by default to avoid spam).
- Payload is Slack-incoming-webhook compatible when the URL host matches hooks.slack.com (wrap as text blocks); otherwise raw JSON. Discord similar simple detection. Keep detection trivial — two well-known hosts, everything else gets the raw shape.
- Existing --notify (osascript) unchanged and composable with --notify-url.
- FAILURES NOTIFY TOO: today only completion is celebrated; interrupted/failed runs are precisely when a away-from-keyboard user needs the ping.
- Delivery: 5s timeout, one retry, failure to deliver logs a warning and never affects run status or exit code.

WHY: long fan-out runs (25-45 min) are exactly the start-and-walk-away workload; non-Mac users and remote/CI usage currently have zero signal.

TESTS (tests/unit/notify.test.js): payload schema per event, slack/discord wrapping by host, timeout + retry-once behavior against a real local http server fixture (node http, not mocked transport), run exit code unaffected by sink failure, step-notify gating flag.

ACCEPTANCE: nax review --notify-url https://hooks.slack.com/... pings on completion AND on ctrl-c interrupt.

title: Temporary Netlify prompt blob offload for oversized fan-in flows
description:
WHY: Large fan-in nax flows can fail on the Netlify API transport with `fork/exec /opt/build-bin/agent-runner: argument list too long`. The observed ideas-flow failure hit at only ~45KB of prompt text because the Netlify agent-runner/orchestrator path passes the assembled prompt through argv when launching the runner. This is a Netlify platform limitation; the correct long-term platform fix is to deliver prompt/context via stdin, a mounted file, a first-class context artifact, or a platform-native reference mechanism. nax cannot change that runner/orchestrator behavior from this repo, so this epic is an explicit temporary compatibility layer, not the permanent ideal architecture.

GOAL: nax must never submit an oversized prompt to the Netlify API transport. For fan-in steps, it should either send the full prompt if under a conservative byte budget, proactively compact below the budget, or offload full prior results to a Netlify Blob and submit only bounded essentials plus fetch instructions. Blob operations must retry transient failures, offloaded context must be verifiable without wasteful false-positive reruns, and temporary blobs must be cleaned up.

CORE INVARIANT: submitted `promptText` UTF-8 bytes must be <= `NAX_SAFE_PROMPT_BYTES`. If nax cannot produce a prompt under that budget and cannot offload, it fails before submission with exact sizes and a sanitized error.

TEMPORARY WORKAROUND BOUNDARY: keep blob logic isolated behind small APIs (`src/netlify-blobs.js`, prompt offload decision helpers, cleanup helpers) so it can be removed or replaced when Netlify supports native large-context delivery. Existing flow semantics (`input: results: all`, submit modes, agents) should not become blob-specific.

MAJOR PARTS: fix the compaction no-op; prove cross-process blob readability; add Netlify Blob CLI wrappers using `blobs:set --input`; add smart retries; implement safe prompt policy and bounded essentials; offload full prior results with marker/sentinel fetch instructions; add cost-aware context fetch confidence classification; persist and clean blob refs; provide orphan sweeping; cover with unit and opt-in real-service e2e tests; tune thresholds against the real ideas flow.
close_reason: All child beads completed: oversized fan-in prompts now use a temporary Netlify Blob offload path with retries, bounded inline essentials, cleanup, tests, and documentation.

title: Fix compact prompt retry so it is byte-budgeted and non-no-op
description:
WHAT: Fix the current compaction safety net so the compact retry is actually smaller than the failed prompt and stays under a conservative byte budget. The observed failure had `promptText` and `compactPromptText` both at 45,075 chars because the current compaction constants allow ~48KB+ of compact prompt (`COMPACT_LOCAL_RESULTS_TOTAL_LIMIT=36000` plus `COMPACT_LOCAL_CONTEXT_CHAR_LIMIT=12000` plus base prompt), which is larger than the prompt that already trips Netlify runner argv limits.

IMPLEMENTATION NOTES:
- Introduce a single byte-based safe budget such as `NAX_SAFE_PROMPT_BYTES` with a conservative default around 16-20KB.
- Measure UTF-8 bytes, not JS string length, when deciding whether a prompt fits.
- Make compact prior results plus compact additional context plus base prompt fit the target end to end.
- Trim results first, then context, but keep prompt instructions and essential metadata intact.
- Keep `src/local-runner.js` `compactPromptForArgumentLimitRetry` guard, but ensure generated compact prompts are reliably smaller.
- Preserve existing behavior byte-for-byte for prompts already under the safe budget where feasible.

TESTS:
- Add a red-green unit/integration case that builds a fan-in step whose full prompt exceeds the safe budget and asserts compact bytes < full bytes and compact bytes <= `NAX_SAFE_PROMPT_BYTES`.
- Cover multi-byte Unicode boundaries so byte counts, not char counts, drive decisions.
- Assert compact retry no longer returns empty solely because compact equals full.

ACCEPTANCE: the previous 45KB failure shape would produce a materially smaller compact prompt below the configured safe byte budget instead of resubmitting the same oversized prompt.
close_reason: Implemented temporary Netlify prompt blob offload with byte budgeting, blob CLI retries, bounded essentials, fetch markers, confidence classification, cleanup/sweep, docs, unit coverage, and opt-in real round-trip probe.

title: Persist blob refs and clean temporary blobs at flow completion
description:
WHAT: Persist blob refs and delete temporary blobs at terminal flow completion with retrying best-effort cleanup.

IMPLEMENTATION NOTES:
- `runState.blobRefs` is the source of truth for blobs created by the current workflow.
- Mirror refs into `.nax/blob-refs.jsonl` append-only registry so cleanup can recover after process crashes/interrupted runs.
- Ref shape should include at least: `{ runId, stepId, store, key, marker, sentinel, createdAt, status, cleanupAttempts, lastCleanupError }`.
- Cleanup should run on terminal flow completion after artifacts are persisted, not after the first terminal agent run, because multiple agents in the same step may share the same prior-results blob.
- Use retrying `deleteBlob`; failures do not fail the workflow result. Persist cleanup warnings on refs and show them in run summaries.
- For interrupted/failed flows, delete only blobs no longer needed by pending/submitted runs; otherwise mark refs `pending-cleanup`.

TESTS:
- Successful terminal flow deletes all registered refs and marks them cleaned.
- Deletion failure is retried, persisted, and surfaced as warning without changing workflow success.
- Multi-agent step does not delete shared blob before all consumers are terminal.
- Interrupted flow preserves refs still needed by pending/submitted runs.
- Registry write is append-safe and tolerant of duplicate deterministic refs on resume.

ACCEPTANCE: normal completed runs leave no temporary prompt blobs behind, and abnormal runs leave enough registry state for later sweep.
close_reason: Implemented temporary Netlify prompt blob offload with byte budgeting, blob CLI retries, bounded essentials, fetch markers, confidence classification, cleanup/sweep, docs, unit coverage, and opt-in real round-trip probe.

title: Add registry-backed orphan sweep for prompt blobs
description:
WHAT: Add orphan sweeping for blob refs left by interrupted/crashed workflows, without assuming the Netlify CLI can list all stores.

IMPLEMENTATION NOTES:
- Implement `nax clean blobs` or a minimal `--clean-blobs` path that reads `.nax/blob-refs.jsonl` and known `runState.blobRefs` records.
- Do not depend on listing all `nax-*` stores; `netlify blobs:list <store>` lists keys inside a known store, not stores globally.
- Eligible refs: terminal/absent run, already no pending/submitted consumers, or older than `NAX_BLOB_CLEANUP_TTL_HOURS` default 24h.
- Deletion uses retrying `deleteBlob`; persistent failures keep refs pending and report sanitized errors.
- Prefer dry-run plan by default if this is integrated into a broader `nax clean`; actual deletion should require an explicit flag if matching existing clean UX.

TESTS:
- Registry with cleaned, pending, stale, and active refs produces correct sweep plan.
- Stale refs delete and update registry status.
- Active/in-flight refs are protected.
- CLI output names store/key/status and sanitized errors, never token/payload.

ACCEPTANCE: a crashed run that left blob refs can be cleaned later from local registry data alone.
close_reason: Implemented temporary Netlify prompt blob offload with byte budgeting, blob CLI retries, bounded essentials, fetch markers, confidence classification, cleanup/sweep, docs, unit coverage, and opt-in real round-trip probe.

title: Test blob offload, verification, retries, cleanup, and real round trip
description:
WHAT: Add comprehensive unit and opt-in real-service e2e coverage for blob offload, retries, context verification, and cleanup.

IMPLEMENTATION NOTES:
- Unit tests should live in the existing `node --test` style and print useful size/decision diagnostics on failure.
- Use fake `runCommand` only for CLI wrapper/retry argument construction; do not fake the cross-process proof.
- Add an opt-in e2e scenario requiring real Netlify credentials/site: local nax writes blob with sentinel, hosted one-shot agent reads it back, echoes marker+sentinel, and cleanup deletes it.
- Add a flow-level oversized fan-in test using generated large prior results. It should assert submitted prompt bytes are under budget, full prose is absent from prompt, blob payload contains full prose and sentinel, and run state records delivery mode.
- Add cleanup e2e where possible: completed flow deletes refs; failed delete leaves pending-cleanup status.

TESTS TO COVER:
- Phase 0 compaction invariant.
- Blob module tempfile usage and sanitization.
- Blob retry classification.
- Offload decision and fallback chain.
- Essentials builder with and without structured JSON.
- Context confidence classifier and rerun guard.
- Registry cleanup and orphan sweep.

ACCEPTANCE: `npm test` covers deterministic unit/integration behavior; opt-in e2e proves the real Netlify cross-process path before the feature is treated as enabled for production use.
close_reason: Implemented temporary Netlify prompt blob offload with byte budgeting, blob CLI retries, bounded essentials, fetch markers, confidence classification, cleanup/sweep, docs, unit coverage, and opt-in real round-trip probe.

title: Tune safe prompt thresholds against real oversized ideas flow
description:
WHAT: Tune byte thresholds against real runs, especially the failed ideas-flow synthesize case, and make resulting diagnostics understandable.

IMPLEMENTATION NOTES:
- Start with conservative `NAX_SAFE_PROMPT_BYTES`, e.g. 16KB, because the observed failure happened below 45KB.
- Run/dry-run the ideas flow shape and record full bytes, compact bytes, offload prompt bytes, blob payload bytes, and selected delivery mode.
- Optionally add a one-off bisection probe to find the actual Netlify runner argv failure ceiling, but do not depend on a generous value; platform behavior may change.
- Ensure console output and workflow artifacts explain when compaction/offload happened and why.
- Validate that structured essentials are sufficient for current ideas-style synthesize output, while arbitrary non-structured flows can still use required policy/manual review.

TESTS/VERIFICATION:
- Dry-run or fixture-generated ideas-flow fan-in reproduces the old 45KB shape and now chooses compact or blob path safely.
- Real run, if credentials are available, completes without E2BIG.
- Summaries include prompt delivery mode and context fetch confidence when applicable.

ACCEPTANCE: thresholds are low enough to avoid the known E2BIG failure and high enough that ordinary small prompts stay inline; the tuning rationale is captured in code comments/docs.
close_reason: Implemented temporary Netlify prompt blob offload with byte budgeting, blob CLI retries, bounded essentials, fetch markers, confidence classification, cleanup/sweep, docs, unit coverage, and opt-in real round-trip probe.

title: Document temporary blob offload behavior, config, and removal path
description:
WHAT: Document the temporary blob-offload workaround, configuration knobs, operational cleanup, and the future removal boundary.

IMPLEMENTATION NOTES:
- Update README/Troubleshooting or relevant docs to explain that blob offload is a temporary workaround for a Netlify platform argv limitation, not the ideal long-term architecture.
- Document `NAX_SAFE_PROMPT_BYTES`, `NAX_PROMPT_BLOB_THRESHOLD`, `NAX_PROMPT_BLOB_DISABLE`, `NAX_BLOB_RETRY_ATTEMPTS`, `NAX_BLOB_CLEANUP_TTL_HOURS`, and `NAX_CONTEXT_FETCH_AUTO_RERUN`.
- Document what users will see: delivery mode, compaction/offload messages, context fetch statuses (`confirmed`, `probable`, `suspect`, `failed`), cleanup warnings, and manual retry guidance.
- Add troubleshooting rows for blob write failure, blob fetch not proven, cleanup pending, and prompt too large even after fallback.
- Note future exit criteria: when Netlify supports native large prompt/context delivery, keep flow semantics unchanged and swap/delete the isolated offload adapter.
- Coordinate with the existing documentation wave bead if needed.

TESTS/VERIFICATION:
- Any docs examples use literal `$NETLIFY_AUTH_TOKEN`/`$SITE_ID`, never real secrets.
- Command/config names match implementation.
- Limitations section does not imply blob offload is permanent platform design.

ACCEPTANCE: a maintainer can operate, debug, disable, and eventually remove the workaround from docs alone.
close_reason: Implemented temporary Netlify prompt blob offload with byte budgeting, blob CLI retries, bounded essentials, fetch markers, confidence classification, cleanup/sweep, docs, unit coverage, and opt-in real round-trip probe.

title: Prove local-write to hosted-runner blob read round trip
description:
WHAT: Prove the gating assumption that nax can write a Netlify Blob locally with the user's PAT and a separate hosted agent-runner pod can read that blob with the scoped DevServer token. The already-proven path only did set/get/list inside one pod/run; the real flow is local nax writes, then a different run reads.

IMPLEMENTATION NOTES:
- Add an opt-in probe command or test helper that writes a known blob under a deterministic temporary store/key for the current Netlify site.
- Spawn a minimal one-shot Netlify agent run whose only job is to run `NETLIFY_SITE_ID="$SITE_ID" netlify blobs:get <store> <key> --auth "$NETLIFY_AUTH_TOKEN"` and echo both the blob value and a marker.
- Verify the result came from the blob, not inline prompt text.
- Clean up the probe blob after the run.
- If the probe 403s, returns empty, or reads from the wrong scope, stop blob wiring work and document the blocker; do not build the rest of the offload path on an unproven cross-principal assumption.

TESTS:
- This is opt-in real-service e2e only, gated by env vars for site/token availability.
- Unit-test only the probe orchestration shape with injected seams; do not fake the cross-process proof.

ACCEPTANCE: a local write followed by a different hosted agent run read succeeds on the same linked site with the scoped runner token, and the test logs store/key, byte sizes, and sanitized errors if it fails.
close_reason: Implemented temporary Netlify prompt blob offload with byte budgeting, blob CLI retries, bounded essentials, fetch markers, confidence classification, cleanup/sweep, docs, unit coverage, and opt-in real round-trip probe.

title: Add isolated Netlify Blob CLI wrapper using tempfile input
description:
WHAT: Add `src/netlify-blobs.js`, a thin Netlify CLI wrapper module for blob set/get/delete. This module is the isolation boundary for the temporary Netlify platform workaround.

IMPLEMENTATION NOTES:
- Implement `setBlob({ store, key, value, siteId, token, cliPath, runCommand })` using a temporary file and `netlify blobs:set <store> <key> --input <path> --auth <token>`.
- Never place blob payloads in argv. This avoids recreating the same prompt-size/argv problem locally.
- Delete the temporary file in `finally` after success or failure.
- Implement `getBlob(...)` for probes/e2e tests and `deleteBlob(...)` for cleanup.
- Pass site identity via `NETLIFY_SITE_ID`/env where appropriate, matching the current CLI usage patterns.
- Sanitize errors so tokens and payload contents cannot be logged.
- Keep the module small, dependency-light, and easy to delete later when Netlify supports native large-context delivery.

TESTS:
- Fake `runCommand` and assert exact argv for set/get/delete, including `--input <tempfile>` for set.
- Assert temp files are removed after success and after failure.
- Assert token values and blob payloads are absent from thrown/logged errors.

ACCEPTANCE: all blob CLI interactions are centralized in `src/netlify-blobs.js`, writes use `--input`, and no caller needs to know how the CLI is invoked.
close_reason: Implemented temporary Netlify prompt blob offload with byte budgeting, blob CLI retries, bounded essentials, fetch markers, confidence classification, cleanup/sweep, docs, unit coverage, and opt-in real round-trip probe.

title: Add smart retries for blob set/get/delete operations
description:
WHAT: Add bounded smart retry behavior for blob set/get/delete calls so transient Netlify or network failures do not immediately kill a long multi-agent workflow.

IMPLEMENTATION NOTES:
- Add a shared retry helper used by `setBlob`, `getBlob`, and `deleteBlob`.
- Default attempts: 3, configurable via `NAX_BLOB_RETRY_ATTEMPTS`.
- Backoff: exponential with jitter, for example 750ms, 1500ms, 3000ms plus jitter.
- Retryable failures: process timeout, `ETIMEDOUT`, `ECONNRESET`, `ECONNREFUSED`, `EAI_AGAIN`, and HTTP 408/409/425/429/500/502/503/504 detected in stderr/stdout/parsed CLI output.
- Non-retryable failures: auth/scope errors, missing site, invalid store/key, malformed command, and other 4xx responses.
- Retry logs should include operation, store, key, attempt number, and sanitized error summary. Never log token values or blob payload excerpts.
- `setBlob` failures after retries feed the prompt fallback chain. `deleteBlob` failures after retries are best-effort cleanup warnings and should be persisted for later sweeping.

TESTS:
- Retryable error then success performs exactly two attempts and returns success.
- Retryable errors through all attempts throw a final sanitized error with operation/store/key context.
- Non-retryable auth/validation error performs one attempt.
- Delete failure does not fail the caller when invoked from cleanup mode but records enough detail for sweep.

ACCEPTANCE: transient blob control-plane failures are retried predictably, and persistent failures surface cleanly to fallback/cleanup code without leaking secrets.
close_reason: Implemented temporary Netlify prompt blob offload with byte budgeting, blob CLI retries, bounded essentials, fetch markers, confidence classification, cleanup/sweep, docs, unit coverage, and opt-in real round-trip probe.

title: Implement safe prompt policy and generic bounded essentials
description:
WHAT: Implement the safe prompt policy and generic bounded essentials builder used before any Netlify API submission.

IMPLEMENTATION NOTES:
- Add helpers that assemble full prompt, compact prompt, and offload prompt candidates while measuring UTF-8 bytes.
- Enforce the invariant that submitted prompt bytes must be <= `NAX_SAFE_PROMPT_BYTES`.
- Below budget: send full inline prompt unchanged.
- Compact under budget: proactively send compacted inline prompt if step policy allows lossy compaction.
- Otherwise: request blob offload.
- Build bounded essentials per source run. Prefer real structured sections via `renderReplyBody(run.resultText, { structuredOnly: true })` when a structured JSON block exists. If missing, include source step, agent, runner/session metadata, result byte count, and a compact head/tail excerpt within a per-run budget. If even that cannot fit, include only metadata plus a pointer that the full report is in the blob.
- Do not make ideas-flow structured JSON a global requirement; arbitrary custom flows may not emit structured sections.

TESTS:
- Full prompt under budget is byte-identical.
- Compact prompt under budget is selected before blob offload.
- Missing structured block still produces useful bounded essentials rather than only a generic placeholder.
- Essentials output remains under budget across many source runs and multi-byte text.
- Console/run-state diagnostics record full bytes, compact bytes, selected tier/path, and what was dropped.

ACCEPTANCE: prompt sizing is proactive, byte-based, deterministic, and reusable by the blob offload path rather than only retry-time repair.
close_reason: Implemented temporary Netlify prompt blob offload with byte budgeting, blob CLI retries, bounded essentials, fetch markers, confidence classification, cleanup/sweep, docs, unit coverage, and opt-in real round-trip probe.

title: Add fetch instruction template plus marker and blob-only sentinel
description:
WHAT: Add the source-controlled fetch-instruction prompt fragment plus deterministic marker and blob-only sentinel generation.

IMPLEMENTATION NOTES:
- Template text should instruct the agent to run exactly: `NETLIFY_SITE_ID="$SITE_ID" netlify blobs:get <store> <key> --auth "$NETLIFY_AUTH_TOKEN"`.
- The prompt must contain literal `$SITE_ID` and `$NETLIFY_AUTH_TOKEN` strings only; never interpolate secret values into prompt text or `.nax` artifacts.
- Generate `NAX-CONTEXT-LOADED <marker>` deterministically from runId+stepId or equivalent stable inputs.
- Generate `NAX-BLOB-SENTINEL <sentinel>` deterministically per offloaded payload, for example runId+stepId+blob payload digest.
- The sentinel must be written only into the blob payload, never into inline essentials, so a final reply containing it is stronger evidence that the agent actually read the blob.
- Template should ask the agent to echo both the marker and sentinel after reading the blob.

TESTS:
- Marker and sentinel are deterministic across resume for the same run/step/payload.
- Sentinel is absent from submitted inline prompt except where the template names the placeholder, and present in the blob payload.
- Prompt fragment contains literal env var names and no token values.
- Store/key/marker/sentinel render correctly for weird but valid step ids.

ACCEPTANCE: offload prompts contain a consistent fetch instruction and verification request, while secrets remain out of artifacts.
close_reason: Implemented temporary Netlify prompt blob offload with byte budgeting, blob CLI retries, bounded essentials, fetch markers, confidence classification, cleanup/sweep, docs, unit coverage, and opt-in real round-trip probe.

title: Wire blob offload into Netlify API fan-in prompt assembly
description:
WHAT: Wire the offload decision into local Netlify API step assembly so oversized fan-in prompts become bounded essentials plus a blob reference before submission.

IMPLEMENTATION NOTES:
- Integrate near the current step assembly in `bin/nax.js` where `sourceRuns`, `roundResults`, `compactRoundResults`, `stepContext`, `promptText`, and `compactPromptText` are built.
- Build and measure full and compact prompt candidates first.
- If neither fits under `NAX_SAFE_PROMPT_BYTES`, create `blobPayload` from full `formatLocalRunResults(sourceRuns)`, prepending the blob-only sentinel.
- Call `setBlob({ store: 'nax-'+runId, key: stepId+'-prior-results', value: blobPayload, ... })` with retry behavior.
- Build final `roundResults` from bounded essentials plus the fetch-instruction template.
- Persist blob metadata on `stepState`/`runState` so resume and cleanup know store/key/marker/sentinel/status.
- Assert final submitted prompt bytes are under `NAX_SAFE_PROMPT_BYTES`; fail before submission if not.
- Respect `NAX_PROMPT_BLOB_DISABLE` by falling back only to safe compacted inline or a pre-submit hard failure.

TESTS:
- Below-budget prompt stays inline and does not call `setBlob`.
- Compact-under-budget prompt sends compacted inline and does not call `setBlob`.
- Oversized prompt calls `setBlob`, omits full prose from submitted prompt, includes bounded essentials and fetch instructions, and records blob refs.
- Dry-run reports offload decisions without performing live blob writes unless explicitly requested.
- Resume reuses deterministic store/key rather than duplicating blob refs.

ACCEPTANCE: the failed ideas-flow synthesize prompt shape would submit a small prompt with inline essentials and a blob fetch instruction instead of a 45KB argv payload.
close_reason: Implemented temporary Netlify prompt blob offload with byte budgeting, blob CLI retries, bounded essentials, fetch markers, confidence classification, cleanup/sweep, docs, unit coverage, and opt-in real round-trip probe.

title: Implement safe fallback chain and pre-submit hard failures
description:
WHAT: Implement the explicit fallback chain and pre-submit failure behavior around blob offload.

IMPLEMENTATION NOTES:
- Fallback chain: full inline if safe -> compacted inline if safe and allowed -> blob offload with retrying `setBlob` -> if `setBlob` fails, compacted inline only if still safe -> otherwise hard fail before submission.
- Hard failure must name step id/title, agent, full prompt bytes, compact prompt bytes, safe byte budget, blob store/key, and sanitized offload error.
- Never silently truncate mid-section without recording what was dropped.
- Ensure `compactPromptText` remains available as a retry rung for argument-list errors, but it must itself be under the safe budget.
- Store final selected delivery mode in run state, e.g. `promptDelivery: inline|compact|blob|failed` and byte metrics for audit/debugging.

TESTS:
- `setBlob` throws after retries and compact prompt is safe -> compact prompt is submitted and warning is recorded.
- `setBlob` throws after retries and compact prompt is too large -> no Netlify agent run is created and error is clear.
- Final prompt invariant catches accidental oversized output after offload assembly.
- `NAX_PROMPT_BLOB_DISABLE` behaves predictably.

ACCEPTANCE: nax never reaches Netlify submission with an oversized prompt, even when blob writes fail.
close_reason: Implemented temporary Netlify prompt blob offload with byte budgeting, blob CLI retries, bounded essentials, fetch markers, confidence classification, cleanup/sweep, docs, unit coverage, and opt-in real round-trip probe.

title: Classify offloaded context fetch confidence without fragile reruns
description:
WHAT: Add cost-aware context fetch confidence classification for offloaded runs. Missing marker alone must not trigger a rerun because agents can read the blob and forget to echo, and synthesize-class reruns are expensive.

IMPLEMENTATION NOTES:
- Parse final agent reply for `NAX-CONTEXT-LOADED <marker>` and blob-only `NAX-BLOB-SENTINEL <sentinel>`.
- Compute `contextFetchStatus`: `confirmed` when marker and/or sentinel is present; `probable` when marker/sentinel are absent but the answer references blob-only details or clearly uses context beyond inline essentials; `suspect` when marker/sentinel are absent and output is generic/context-starved; `failed` when explicit blob fetch/auth/read errors appear.
- Persist `contextFetchStatus`, `contextFetchSignals`, and `contextFetchConfirmed` where confirmed/probable count as true.
- Add `contextFetchPolicy: optional|required` per offloaded run. Optional policy warns for weak evidence. Required policy marks suspect/failed for manual review by default.
- Add `NAX_CONTEXT_FETCH_AUTO_RERUN`, default false. Only when enabled and required-policy status is suspect/failed should nax attempt one stricter fetch-first rerun. Still never rerun on missing marker alone.
- Surface suspect/failed in summaries and visualize UI so users can spot under-informed syntheses.

TESTS:
- Marker/sentinel present -> confirmed.
- Missing marker but reply cites blob-only sentinel/details -> probable.
- Generic answer only restating inline essentials -> suspect.
- Explicit CLI/auth/read error in reply -> failed.
- Required policy with auto-rerun disabled never reruns and records manual-review status.
- Required policy with auto-rerun enabled reruns at most once and fails if still suspect/failed.

ACCEPTANCE: verification catches real ignored/failing blob fetches without burning credits on marker false negatives.
close_reason: Implemented temporary Netlify prompt blob offload with byte budgeting, blob CLI retries, bounded essentials, fetch markers, confidence classification, cleanup/sweep, docs, unit coverage, and opt-in real round-trip probe.

title: PR review output: post consensus findings as a PR review
description:
WHAT: nax handoff --to pr-review [--pr 123]: take findings.json from the selected workflow and post a single GitHub PR review (gh api) containing: summary comment (consensus overview + workflow link) + one inline comment per finding that maps to a file/line in the PR diff (findings carry files[]; line mapping best-effort via diff hunk search for the finding's quoted code when present, else file-level comment). Findings not mappable to the diff appended to the summary body under 'Outside this diff'.

DEFAULT PR: if the workflow was run with --branch '#123' that PR; else the open PR for the workflow branch; else require --pr.

IDEMPOTENCY: review body carries marker '<!-- nax-review:<workflowRunId> -->'; existing marker -> refuse with link unless --force (which posts a fresh review, never edits).

WHY: closes the loop for PR-centric teams — multi-agent consensus lands exactly where humans already review, instead of a side-channel issue. Event-comment mode (review type COMMENT, never REQUEST_CHANGES) keeps nax advisory, not gatekeeping — that is a deliberate posture decision.

TESTS: handoff-targets tests extend: payload shape (review body, inline comment file/line), unmappable-finding fallback, idempotency refusal, default-PR resolution chain. gh exec seam with fixture payloads captured from real gh output.

ACCEPTANCE: after nax review --branch '#123', nax handoff --to pr-review posts one review with inline comments on the PR; rerun refuses politely.

title: Visualize Phase 3: safe real run submission from UI
description: Enable real workflow submission from the UI through a token-protected localhost API. This phase may initially delegate to `node bin/nax.js run ...` with argv arrays, streams stdout/stderr/process lifecycle through server-sent events, guards duplicate submissions, and recovers visible state from `.nax/` after refresh.
close_reason: Completed Phase 3 safe real run submission from UI with token guardrails, subprocess run endpoint, SSE/cancel, confirmation UI, state recovery, and tests.

title: Add token-protected mutating API guardrails
description:
WHAT: Harden the visualize server for mutating run-control endpoints. Require a per-process token on every POST/DELETE-style endpoint, validate origin/host assumptions appropriate for localhost, bind to `127.0.0.1` by default, and keep all workflow ids/options validated against loaded workflows.

WHY: Real run submission can create GitHub issues, trigger GitHub Actions, and spend Netlify agent credits. Localhost is still a privileged control plane and must be treated accordingly.

ACCEPTANCE: Mutating requests without token fail. Read-only routes remain accessible for the loaded UI. Workflow id, transport, branch, model, step, and from-step validation happens before any child process or runner action. Tests cover missing token, bad token, and valid token paths.
close_reason: Mutating visualize endpoints now require the per-process token; server validates workflow ids, transports, step/fromStep, and model names before spawning commands.

title: Add safe subprocess-backed real workflow run endpoint
description:
WHAT: Add `POST /api/workflows/:id/runs` using safe subprocess delegation as the first real-run implementation. Build argv arrays directly for `node bin/nax.js run <flowId> --project-root <root> --transport <transport> --branch <branch> --force ...`; never accept or execute arbitrary shell command strings.

DETAILS: Record process id, run request id, workflow id, command args, startedAt, exitedAt, exit code, stdout/stderr buffers or event references, and current status. Integrate with existing run-lock and fail-fast validation work when available so duplicate/corrupt runs are avoided before remote side effects happen.

ACCEPTANCE: A real run can be started from the UI in a wired repo. Failed process start and nonzero exit both return clear lifecycle state. The endpoint refuses unsupported transports/options before spawning.
close_reason: Added POST /api/workflows/:id/runs backed by safe argv-array subprocess delegation, active run registry, duplicate active workflow guard, and command lifecycle metadata.

title: Stream run lifecycle events and support cancellation
description:
WHAT: Add server-sent events for run lifecycle: stdout chunks, stderr chunks, process started, process exited, run-state refresh, and error events. Add cancellation endpoint for still-running child processes with clear semantics and status reporting.

WHY: Long agent workflows need live feedback. A browser refresh should not be the only way to know whether the run is still alive, and operators need a controlled cancel path for accidental submissions.

ACCEPTANCE: UI can subscribe to `/api/runs/:runId/events` and receive ordered lifecycle events. Cancel sends a termination signal, updates status, and reports if the process was already complete. Event streams close cleanly at terminal state.
close_reason: Added /api/runs/:id/events SSE replay/live stream and /api/runs/:id/cancel endpoint with event recording and client cleanup.

title: Add confirmed real-run UI and duplicate submission guard
description:
WHAT: Add UI real-run controls behind an explicit confirmation flow. The confirmation must show workflow title/id, project root, branch, transport, selected models, context presence, and the fact that the run can create remote work and spend credits. Prevent accidental duplicate submissions for the same active workflow unless explicitly confirmed.

WHY: The UI should make starting a real workflow easier but not casual. Dry-run remains the safer default action.

ACCEPTANCE: Real run button is visually distinct from dry-run. Confirmation modal summarizes exact options. Duplicate active run attempts are blocked or require a second explicit confirmation. Lifecycle events update the run panel.
close_reason: Added confirmed real-run UI button separate from dry-run; confirmation names branch, transport, models, and remote credit side effects; output streams into a run panel.

title: Recover run status from .nax workflow state
description:
WHAT: Read `.nax/workflows` state after run submission and on browser refresh. Expose run list/detail APIs sufficient for the UI to recover active or recently completed run status from durable state instead of only in-memory child process records.

DETAILS: Use existing `listRunStates`, `workflowStatePath`, and artifact helper semantics where possible. Distinguish in-memory active process status from durable workflow status. Link to workflow summary artifact when present.

ACCEPTANCE: Refreshing the browser during or after a run can show known run status and artifact paths. Completed/failed/interrupted states display correctly when read only from `.nax/`.
close_reason: Added /api/runs and /api/runs/:id durable state reads from .nax/workflows with public run-state serialization.

title: Test token-protected run control, SSE, cancel, and recovery
description:
WHAT: Test real-run control without requiring real Netlify or GitHub side effects in CI. Cover token enforcement, argv construction, process lifecycle capture, SSE event ordering, cancellation, duplicate guard behavior, and refresh recovery from fixture `.nax/workflows` state.

DETAILS: Use local child-process fixtures or injected spawn seams. Tests should assert that user inputs are never shell-concatenated and that no remote command is attempted for invalid inputs.

ACCEPTANCE: Server tests exercise start, event stream, cancel, process failure, and state recovery paths. Failure output includes enough lifecycle details to diagnose broken run-control behavior.
close_reason: Added run-control tests for token enforcement, run start, event replay, cancel response, and durable .nax workflow state recovery; reran browser smoke.

title: Runs API pagination for durable dashboard history
description:
WHY: The dashboard currently answers GET /api/runs by reading every durable workflow state from .nax/workflows, serializing every historical run, and storing all runs in one React Query list. That is correct for small histories but makes dashboard startup and live invalidation increasingly expensive as local run history grows. Users usually need active runs, the latest durable runs, and a simple way to request older history on demand.

GOAL: Add conservative pagination for durable runs while preserving the current Recent Runs UX. Active in-memory runs must remain visible and unpaginated. Durable history should default to a generous first page, expose metadata that tells the UI whether more history exists, and support opaque cursors for loading older pages.

IMPORTANT DESIGN CORRECTION: Do not implement pagination by calling listRunStates(projectRoot) and slicing afterward. listRunStates currently parses every workflow.json before sorting. That would reduce response size but still leave dashboard startup doing unlimited filesystem and JSON work. The implementation must introduce a paged durable-state listing path that enumerates/sorts state files cheaply, slices by cursor/limit, and parses only the selected page.

API SHAPE: GET /api/runs?limit=50 and GET /api/runs?limit=50&cursor=<opaque>. New responses should include active, durable, and pagination metadata: durableLimit, durableOffset, durableTotal, nextCursor, hasMore. Existing clients that ignore pagination should still parse the response, but once enabled they receive only the first durable page.

NON-GOALS: No database. No storage-format migration. No active-run pagination. No search/filtering. No Recent Runs redesign. No archival/retention policy.

SOURCE PLAN: docs/ai/plans/nax-runs-api-pagination.md.

ACCEPTANCE: /api/runs supports limit/cursor, default dashboard load no longer parses/serializes unlimited durable history, Recent Runs can load older durable runs, active runs remain visible, cache invalidation remains scoped, typecheck/tests/dashboard build pass.
close_reason: Completed durable runs pagination epic: paged pre-parse state listing, /api/runs cursor contract, typed client/infinite query cache bridge, Recent Runs Load older UI, tests, docs, and validation.

title: Paged durable workflow state listing without parsing all history
description:
SCOPE: Add a reusable helper in src/run-state.js for paged durable workflow state listing. The helper must let /api/runs fetch only one durable page without parsing every workflow.json under .nax/workflows.

WHY: The markdown plan's simple pseudo-code slices after listRunStates(projectRoot), but listRunStates parses every workflow state before sorting. That would not satisfy the core performance goal. This bead is the foundation that makes pagination real rather than cosmetic.

IMPLEMENTATION DIRECTION:
- Keep existing listRunStates/listWorkflowStates behavior for current callers.
- Add a helper such as listWorkflowStatePage(projectRoot, { limit, offset }) or listRunStatePage(projectRoot, options).
- Reuse cleanupLegacyRunsDir(projectRoot) so legacy .nax/runs migration behavior remains intact.
- Enumerate .nax/workflows child directories and workflow.json files.
- Sort newest-first using cheap file metadata before parsing. Prefer workflow.json mtimeMs plus a deterministic path/name tie-breaker. It is acceptable if this differs slightly from updatedAt ordering for old files, but document the tradeoff in code because this is a local developer dashboard.
- Slice the sorted file list by offset/limit.
- Parse only the selected workflow.json files and attach dir just like listWorkflowStates.
- Return enough metadata for the server to report durableTotal and hasMore.
- Skip unreadable/invalid selected JSON files consistently with listWorkflowStates; tests should make the intended behavior explicit.

TESTS: Extend tests/unit/run-state.test.js or an equivalent focused unit test. Cover empty histories, newest-first ordering, limit/offset behavior, total count, parsing only selected files if practical, and invalid JSON handling. Avoid brittle sleeps; set mtimes explicitly with fs.utimesSync when testing ordering.

VALIDATION: node --test tests/unit/run-state.test.js and npm run typecheck pass.
close_reason: Added listWorkflowStatePage in src/run-state.js so /api/runs can enumerate/sort durable state files cheaply, slice by limit/offset, and parse only the selected workflow.json page; focused run-state tests pass.

title: Add /api/runs durable pagination contract
description:
SCOPE: Update src/dashboard/server.js so GET /api/runs accepts durable-history pagination parameters and returns pagination metadata while preserving auth, active-run behavior, and structured errors.

REQUEST CONTRACT:
- GET /api/runs uses default durable limit 50.
- GET /api/runs?limit=<n> accepts positive integer limits. Invalid/missing limits fall back to default. Limits above the max clamp to 200.
- GET /api/runs?cursor=<opaque> decodes a cursor returned by a prior response. Cursor internals may initially be base64url JSON such as { offset: 50 }, but clients must treat it as opaque.

RESPONSE CONTRACT:
- active: all current in-memory dashboard runs, unpaginated.
- durable: only the selected durable page from the paged state-listing helper.
- pagination: { durableLimit, durableOffset, durableTotal, nextCursor, hasMore }. New server responses should always include this object.

ERROR CONTRACT:
- Invalid cursor returns requestError(400, 'invalid_cursor', 'Invalid runs cursor.').
- Auth behavior and security headers must remain unchanged.

IMPLEMENTATION NOTES:
- Add small helpers near dashboard route helpers: parsePositiveInteger, encodeRunsCursor, decodeRunsCursor, paginatedDurableRuns or equivalent.
- Use the paged run-state helper from the prerequisite bead. Do not call listRunStates(projectRoot).map(publicRunState) for /api/runs first-page behavior.
- Offset cursors can shift if history mutates while a user pages. That is acceptable for this local admin dashboard; add a concise comment so future readers understand the tradeoff.

TESTS: Extend tests/unit/dashboard-server.test.js. Cover default metadata, limit=1 returns one durable run and hasMore true, nextCursor retrieves the next durable run, max limit clamp, invalid cursor structured 400, auth still required, and active runs remain present independent of durable pagination where feasible.

VALIDATION: node --test tests/unit/dashboard-server.test.js and npm run typecheck pass.
close_reason: Updated /api/runs to accept limit/cursor, return durable pagination metadata, reject invalid cursors with structured 400 errors, and use the paged run-state helper instead of listRunStates().map(...); dashboard server tests pass.

title: Add typed runs pagination client contract
description:
SCOPE: Update dashboard web types and API helpers so the client can request paginated durable runs without changing the UI yet.

TYPE CHANGES:
- Add RunsPagination to src/dashboard/web/src/types.ts with durableLimit, durableOffset, durableTotal, nextCursor, hasMore.
- Extend RunsResponse to include optional pagination for transitional compatibility. New server responses should include it, but typed client helpers must tolerate old responses in tests and local development.

API CHANGES:
- Change listRuns to accept options: { limit?: number; cursor?: string } = {}.
- Build query params only when values are present; preserve existing listRuns() behavior for first-page callers.
- Keep auth/session bootstrap behavior unchanged.

QUERY KEY CHANGES:
- Add an explicit infinite/paginated runs key that does not collide with per-run entity keys. Prefer dashboardQueryKeys.runs({ limit }) or dashboardQueryKeys.runsInfinite(limit) over cursor-specific top-level keys if useInfiniteQuery will own page params.
- Preserve broad invalidation semantics: invalidating dashboardQueryKeys.runs() or a shared prefix should refresh the first/latest list without invalidating dashboardQueryKeys.run(runId) entity entries.

TESTS: Extend tests/unit/dashboard-query-cache.test.ts for query key hierarchy and non-collision. Add API URL-construction coverage if there is an existing fetch/api test harness; otherwise cover this through the later hook/UI tests.

VALIDATION: node --import tsx --test tests/unit/dashboard-query-cache.test.ts and npm run typecheck pass.
close_reason: Added typed RunsPagination/RunsListData contracts, optional listRuns({ limit, cursor }) query construction, and explicit paginated runs query keys; typecheck and focused query tests pass.

title: Introduce infinite runs query and page flattening helpers
description:
SCOPE: Replace the dashboard's flat first-page run-list query with a TanStack Query infinite-query path that models cursor pagination directly while preserving the existing flat DashboardRun[] shape for most App/RecentRuns consumers.

CURRENT STATE: useRunsQuery currently returns DashboardRun[] from runsFromResponse(await listRuns()). RecentRuns receives that flat array. Cache helpers also assume dashboardQueryKeys.runs() stores DashboardRun[].

IMPLEMENTATION DIRECTION:
- Add useRunsInfiniteQuery({ limit }) or update useRunsQuery to wrap useInfiniteQuery behind a compatibility return shape. Prefer a new hook if that keeps risk lower.
- Query function should call listRuns({ limit, cursor: pageParam || undefined }).
- getNextPageParam should use response.pagination?.nextCursor.
- Flatten active and durable pages into a stable list for display. Active runs should come from the first/latest page. Durable runs should come from all loaded pages.
- Dedupe by canonical run id using existing runId/runIdentifier logic so active+durable overlap and page-shift duplicates do not duplicate rows.
- Expose hasNextPage, isFetchingNextPage, fetchNextPage, and pagination counts needed by Recent Runs.

CACHE COMPATIBILITY: Be explicit about whether dashboardQueryKeys.runs() stores infinite query data or remains a flat compatibility cache. Do not silently keep upsertRunInDashboardCache writing DashboardRun[] into a key now owned by useInfiniteQuery. This can be solved by changing cache writes in the cache-bridge bead.

TESTS: Add unit tests for flattening multiple RunsResponse pages, deduping active/durable/page duplicates, missing pagination compatibility, and next cursor extraction if helperized.

VALIDATION: node --import tsx --test tests/unit/dashboard-query-cache.test.ts and npm run typecheck pass.
close_reason: Converted the dashboard runs hook to TanStack useInfiniteQuery, flattening active plus durable pages with dedupe and exposing hasMore/count metadata for Recent Runs; focused query tests pass.

title: Adapt dashboard run cache bridge for paginated runs
description:
SCOPE: Review and update dashboard cache helpers and live-event bridge behavior so paginated runs do not corrupt React Query cache shapes and live updates still refresh the latest list correctly.

CURRENT RISK: upsertRunInDashboardCache currently does queryClient.setQueryData<DashboardRun[]>(dashboardQueryKeys.runs(), ...). If dashboardQueryKeys.runs() becomes a useInfiniteQuery cache, this direct flat-array write is wrong.

IMPLEMENTATION OPTIONS:
1. Prefer simplicity: write updated runs to per-run entity keys via dashboardQueryKeys.run(id), then invalidate the runs-list query so the latest first page refetches.
2. If direct list patching is still needed for responsiveness, patch only the first infinite page while preserving InfiniteData<RunsResponse> structure and pagination metadata.

REQUIREMENTS:
- invalidateRunViews still invalidates runs list, run entity, run graph, and run details for the affected run.
- invalidateDashboardLists still invalidates the runs-list query without matching per-run entity keys.
- applyRunnerEventToDashboardCache and related bridges must not assume runs-list data is a flat DashboardRun[].
- Completed active runs should appear after the first-page refetch and should dedupe against any loaded older page.
- Older loaded pages should not be aggressively refetched on every live event beyond normal TanStack Query behavior.

TESTS: Extend tests/unit/dashboard-query-cache.test.ts and tests/unit/query-event-bridge.test.ts. Cover cache shape preservation, list invalidation, per-run entity update, active-to-durable completion dedupe, and non-collision with run entity keys.

VALIDATION: node --import tsx --test tests/unit/dashboard-query-cache.test.ts tests/unit/query-event-bridge.test.ts and npm run typecheck pass.
close_reason: Updated dashboard cache helpers and event bridge expectations so individual run upserts update run entity caches and invalidate the paginated runs list instead of writing flat arrays into an infinite-query key; cache and event bridge tests pass.
