title: Add Recent Runs Load older UI
description:
SCOPE: Update Recent Runs and its App.tsx integration so users can load older durable history on demand while keeping the current dense operational layout.

UX REQUIREMENTS:
- Initial dashboard load shows active runs plus the latest durable page, same visual list as today.
- If pagination.hasMore / hasNextPage is true, show a compact Load older button at the bottom of Recent Runs.
- Button text: Load older.
- Loading state should be confined to that button while fetching additional pages.
- Do not show cursor values.
- Do not add a toolbar or redesign the panel.
- Optional compact count text is acceptable if it fits cleanly, e.g. Showing 50 of 312 saved runs. Avoid crowding the header.
- Active runs remain at the top after older durable pages are loaded.

IMPLEMENTATION NOTES:
- Add explicit props to RecentRuns: hasMore, loadingMore, onLoadMore, durableShownCount, durableTotal, or an equivalent small object.
- Keep presentational responsibilities in RecentRuns; data fetching should stay in query hooks/App integration.
- Maintain existing actions: load run graph, open details, resume run.

TESTS: Add focused component/unit coverage if an existing React test harness exists. Otherwise extend Playwright dashboard smoke only if cheap and reliable: seed more durable runs than the page limit, assert Load older appears, click it, assert older rows append and existing actions still work. Do not block implementation solely on Playwright if server/client unit coverage is strong.

VALIDATION: npm run dashboard:build, npm test or focused tests, and dashboard smoke if UI e2e coverage is added.
close_reason: Added Recent Runs Load older UI with paginated count text and wired it to fetchNextPage; dashboard build and focused Playwright Load older smoke pass.

title: End-to-end validation and documentation for runs pagination
description:
SCOPE: Finish the pagination work by validating the full server/client/UI path, updating implementation docs, and recording the behavior future contributors should preserve.

VALIDATION CHECKLIST:
- /api/runs default response includes pagination metadata.
- /api/runs?limit=1 returns one durable run when durable history exists and hasMore true when appropriate.
- nextCursor fetches the next durable page.
- invalid cursor returns structured 400 invalid_cursor.
- active runs are always included independent of durable page size.
- Recent Runs shows initial page quickly and can append older rows.
- Cache invalidation does not collide with per-run entity caches.
- No source/test code assumes /api/runs returns all durable history by default.

DOCS:
- Update docs/ai/plans/nax-runs-api-pagination.md from draft to implemented notes or add an implementation notes section.
- Document the key invariant: /api/runs must not call listRunStates and then slice; it must page before parsing durable JSON.
- Note offset cursor tradeoff: local admin dashboard can tolerate minor duplication/omission if history mutates during paging, and the client dedupes by canonical run id.

COMMANDS TO RUN BEFORE CLOSE:
- npm run typecheck
- npm run check
- npm test or focused server/client route tests if full test cost is too high
- npm run check:jsdoc-types
- npm run dashboard:build because this includes a UI change
- npm run dashboard:smoke if Playwright coverage is added or touched

ACCEPTANCE: All pagination child beads are complete, docs reflect shipped behavior, validation commands pass, and this epic can be closed without needing to consult the original markdown plan.
close_reason: Validated paginated durable listing, /api/runs contract, client infinite query/cache bridge, Load older UI, documentation, unit tests, dashboard build, full check, npm test, and dashboard smoke.

title: Pluggable agent registry: config-driven agent list beyond claude/gemini/codex
description:
WHAT: Today the agent set is hardcoded in multiple places (flow defaults, validation, title casing, flavor messages, runner mention building). README Limitations admits it: 'Three agents. Adding more requires extending the runner action and the flow schema.' Centralize into src/agent-registry.js:
- built-ins: claude, gemini, codex with displayName, mention/model identifier per transport, flavor messages key
- nax.config.json extension: agents: { grok: { displayName: 'Grok', runnerModel: '...', transports: [netlify-api] } } — validated at load (unknown fields rejected, transports subset checked)
- flow-schema validates step agents against the merged registry, so a project that registers an agent can use it in project flows immediately; bundled flows keep using built-ins only
- doctor gains a check: each registered agent's transport support vs configured transports (warn if an agent is unusable everywhere)

HONESTY GUARD: nax cannot make the Netlify runner support a model it does not support — registry validation should verify the runner accepts the model identifier where the API allows discovery, else mark the agent 'unverified, will fail at submit if unsupported' in doctor output. This bead is the CLI-side plumbing so that the day the runner adds a model, users add 4 lines of config instead of waiting for a nax release.

TESTS (tests/unit/agent-registry.test.js): merge precedence (config cannot redefine built-ins without explicit override: true), validation rejections with messages, flow-schema integration (project flow with registered agent lints clean; unknown agent names the registry as fix), title/mention building for a registered agent, doctor check outputs.

ACCEPTANCE: register a hypothetical agent in config, reference it in a project flow, nax lint passes, dry-run plan renders it; removing config makes lint fail with a clear message.

title: Harden prompt delivery: file-based prompts + tiered context compaction
description:
WHY: The #1 documented bug (docs/ai/bugs/netlify-agent-runner-argument-list-too-long.md) is follow-up sessions failing at runner launch because large prompts (prior-round results embedded) travel via argv/env and blow OS ARG_MAX. Separately, the github-actions transport enforces an ~80KB env string budget at plan time and just errors, forcing users to hand-trim context. Both are symptoms of one root cause: prompt size handling is ad-hoc and transport-specific.

GOAL: One shared prompt-budget module with progressive degradation, plus a root-cause delivery fix so big prompts never hit argv limits on the netlify-api transport.

DESIGN:
- New src/prompt-budget.js: knows each transport's hard limit (gh env ~80KB safe max, runner argv limit), measures a built prompt, and applies compaction tiers in order until it fits: tier 0 = full; tier 1 = structured-findings-only from prior rounds (drop prose); tier 2 = links-only ledger (issue URLs + one-line summaries, instruct agent to fetch); tier 3 = hard error with exact byte counts and the flag to fix it.
- netlify-api transport: deliver prompt content via a file written to the repo checkout (or stdin) instead of argv/env where the runner API supports it; prompt text becomes a pointer + payload.
- Both transports report which tier was applied in console output and in workflow.json (context.compactionTier) so results are interpretable later.

ACCEPTANCE: A review flow whose round-2 prompt embeds >200KB of round-1 results completes on both transports without manual trimming; the applied tier is visible in run output and artifacts.

SERVES: reliability pillar — 'Resume over re-run' philosophy extends to 'degrade over die'.

title: Build src/prompt-budget.js shared budget + compaction tier module
File-based prompt delivery for netlify-api follow-up runs
description:
WHAT: Root-cause fix for the argument-list-too-long failure. When submitting follow-up sessions through the Netlify Agent Runner API (src/local-runner.js submitLocalAgentRun), stop passing the full prompt through argv/env once it exceeds a conservative threshold (~100KB). Instead write the full prompt body to a temp artifact the runner can read, and submit a short pointer prompt: 'Your full instructions are in <path>; read that file first and follow it exactly.'

INVESTIGATE FIRST: confirm what channels the runner API actually supports for large payloads (request body field limits vs argv pass-through into the runner process). The bug doc notes the failure is at runner process launch, i.e., the orchestrator-side submission succeeds but spawning chokes — so the fix likely lives in HOW the action invokes the agent CLI, and our lever is keeping the submitted prompt small. If we cannot change the runner side, the pointer-file approach via a committed-or-pushed context file (or issue body, which has no argv problem) is the fallback: for action=comment steps the content already lives in the GitHub comment, so the runner prompt can reference the comment URL instead of embedding.

ACCEPTANCE: the repro in docs/ai/bugs/netlify-agent-runner-argument-list-too-long.md no longer fails; a follow-up step with 300KB of embedded prior results launches successfully.

DEPENDS: uses prompt-budget module to decide when to switch delivery modes.

title: Wire prompt-budget tiers into both transports + retry path
description:
WHAT: Replace plan-time 80KB error in bin/nax.js (github-actions path) and the retry-time prompt compaction in src/local-runner.js with calls to prompt-budget fitPrompt. Record the applied tier into run-state (workflow.json context.compactionTier and per-run promptTier) via src/run-state.js, and surface it in console output ('context compacted to tier 1: dropped 142KB of prior-round prose') and in workflow summary.md via src/workflow-artifacts.js.

UX: warnings should name exactly what was dropped and how to override (--no-fetch-results, --pr-limit N, --context-file). Never silently truncate mid-section — tiers drop whole named sections only.

ACCEPTANCE: oversized prompt on github-actions degrades to tier 1/2 with a clear console line instead of erroring; identical compaction behavior on first submit and on retry.

title: Tests: prompt-budget unit + oversized-prompt integration coverage
description:
WHAT: Comprehensive tests with detailed logging so post-implementation verification is trivial.

UNIT (tests/unit/prompt-budget.test.js, node --test like existing suite):
- tier 0 pass-through is byte-identical for under-budget prompts
- tier 1 drops exactly the sections marked droppable, preserves structured findings blocks
- tier 2 produces links-only ledger containing every source issue URL
- tier 3 throws with byte counts and remediation hints in the message (assert message content)
- boundary cases: exactly at safeMax, 1 byte over, empty sections, unicode multi-byte counting

INTEGRATION (extend tests/unit/flow-execution.test.js patterns): drive a fake flow whose round-1 results are generated >200KB strings and assert (a) the built round-2 submission is under the transport budget, (b) workflow.json records compactionTier, (c) console output (captured) names the dropped sections. Use real generated data, not mocks of the budget logic itself.

LOGGING: each test logs input sizes, chosen tier, output sizes so failures are diagnosable from CI output alone.

title: Dashboard route spine for workflow, prompt, and run inspection
description:
WHY: The dashboard has grown from a local visualizer into the main UI for browsing workflows, configuring dry-runs/runs, inspecting completed jobs, reading prompt inputs, and eventually launching agent runs from local CLI, a desktop companion, and a hosted web app. Today that intent is encoded across local React state (`selectedWorkflowId`, `selectedRunId`, `selectedNode`, `detailsModalContext`, `promptModalStepId`) plus manual `window.history.replaceState`, so workflow-definition mode and run-inspection mode can drift into each other.

GOAL: Introduce a typed, mode-aware dashboard route spine backed by TanStack Query. Routes should make workflow definition browsing, prompt inspection, run inspection, and run details deep-linkable and browser-navigation-friendly while preserving existing UI behavior.

TARGET ROUTES:
- /workflows/$workflowId
- /workflows/$workflowId/steps/$stepId
- /workflows/$workflowId/prompts
- /workflows/$workflowId/prompts/$stepId
- /runs/$runId
- /runs/$runId/steps/$stepId
- /runs/$runId/steps/$stepId/agents/$agent

DEPLOYMENT PRINCIPLE: The same React UI must work against a dashboard API in local CLI mode, desktop companion mode, and hosted web mode. Local-only features such as opening files in an editor must be capability-gated; hosted web must call backend APIs for dry-run/run actions and must not assume local filesystem access.

SOURCE PLAN: docs/ai/plans/nax-dashboard-routing.md
close_reason: Completed the dashboard route spine and all child beads for Query ownership cleanup, deployment capabilities, configurable API transport, TanStack Router routes for workflows/prompts/runs/details, legacy state cleanup, portability hardening, tests, and docs.

title: Dashboard Query ownership cleanup before Router
description:
WHY: TanStack Query is already installed and should be the server-state boundary before Router lands. Router should select URL state; Query should own health, workflow lists, workflow graphs, runs, run graphs, run details, mutations, cache upserts, and invalidations.

SCOPE:
- Audit App.tsx and dashboard components for remaining direct route-adjacent API calls that should use existing Query hooks/mutations.
- Prefer deriving graph rendering data from `useWorkflowGraphQuery(workflowId)` or `useRunGraphQuery(runId)` instead of mirroring `graph` local state where practical.
- Keep event-stream output and `liveRunReducer` local because those are append/live UI state, not purely server cache.
- Keep `selectedWorkflowId` and `selectedRunId` local only until Router replaces them.
- Ensure run-start, dry-run, cancellation, follow-up, and human-review actions use mutation hooks and update/invalidate `dashboardQueryKeys` consistently.

ACCEPTANCE:
- Route-adjacent UI code uses Query hooks/mutations rather than raw `fetch` or ad hoc API calls.
- Existing dry-run/run/recent-run behavior is unchanged.
- `npm run dashboard:build` passes.
- Add or update focused tests for cache helpers if behavior changes.
close_reason: App now derives graph data from Query results and keeps route-adjacent API access behind existing Query hooks/mutations; check/build/test pass.

title: Harden dashboard for local CLI desktop and hosted web deployment
description:
WHY: The dashboard should not be boxed into local CLI forever. Hosted web and desktop companion deployments need the same UI to work against backend APIs without local filesystem assumptions.

SCOPE:
- Replace local-path-only artifact assumptions with stable artifact ids or URLs where backend payloads can provide them.
- Ensure dry-run/run/follow-up/cancel/review actions always go through backend API mutations.
- Make local filesystem features disabled or transformed into web-safe alternatives in hosted mode.
- Validate event streaming semantics for local and hosted APIs; document SSE/fetch streaming requirements.
- Ensure authentication strategy is not tied to query-token bootstrap for hosted mode; document needed backend auth/CORS/CSRF decisions.

ACCEPTANCE:
- Hosted mode can browse workflows, prompts, runs, and run details without local file access.
- Hosted mode can start dry-run/run actions through backend mutations when `canDryRun`/`canStartRuns` are true.
- Absolute local paths are redacted/transformed or capability-protected in hosted API payloads.
- Developer docs describe local CLI, desktop, and hosted configuration.
close_reason: Hosted/desktop/local capabilities documented and enforced; run actions use backend mutations, local file opens are gated, API base is configurable.

title: Tests for dashboard routing prompts run details and capability modes
description:
WHY: Routing touches interaction behavior, query enablement, modal state, browser navigation, React Flow remount behavior, and deployment capability gating. This needs explicit tests so the route migration does not regress the current UI.

SCOPE:
- Add focused unit/component tests where practical for route-derived selection helpers, capability-gated artifact actions, and query key usage.
- Extend Playwright dashboard smoke tests for:
  - workflow route loads in configure mode
  - card click updates Inspector, not details modal
  - agent pill toggles model selection on workflow routes
  - prompt route deep link opens `WorkflowPromptModal`
  - run route opens inspect mode
  - run step/agent routes open `RunDetailsModal`
  - browser back/forward closes/reopens modal routes correctly
  - hosted capability fixture disables local file opening
- Include detailed logging/screenshots on failure because these are visual workflow regressions.

ACCEPTANCE:
- New tests fail against pre-router local-state behavior where meaningful and pass after migration.
- `npm run dashboard:build` passes.
- `npm run dashboard:smoke` or equivalent targeted Playwright command covers route behavior.
close_reason: Added and verified dashboard routing/capability coverage, including route parser/query ownership unit tests, hosted capability server checks, and the full dashboard Playwright smoke suite for routed workflow, run details, and follow-up composer behavior. Verified with npm run dashboard:build, npm run dashboard:smoke, npm run check, and npm test.

title: Document dashboard routing state ownership and deployment modes
description:
WHY: Dashboard routing and deployment portability add new developer/operator surface area. Future maintainers need to know which state belongs to Router, Query, React local state, and backend capabilities.

SCOPE:
- Update dashboard architecture docs or README sections to explain Router vs Query vs local state ownership.
- Document route patterns and deep-link behavior for workflow, prompt, run, and run-detail routes.
- Document local CLI, desktop companion, and hosted web deployment modes.
- Document capability flags and how UI should behave when capabilities are absent.
- Include a migration note for legacy `?workflow=<id>` compatibility.

ACCEPTANCE:
- Docs are accurate after implementation.
- Routes and capability flags are discoverable to future agents/humans.
- No stale statement says prompt modal or run details are local-only after routing lands.
close_reason: Updated docs/ai/plans/nax-dashboard-routing.md with implemented route ownership, Query ownership, prompt/details routing, capability flags, configurable API base, local CLI/desktop/hosted web deployment notes, and legacy workflow query compatibility.

title: Add dashboard deployment capabilities and gate local-only actions
description:
WHY: The dashboard must run locally via CLI, in a desktop companion, and as a hosted web app. React components currently have local-machine assumptions such as opening local files and using same-origin `/api`. Those assumptions need to become explicit backend capabilities so the same UI can safely degrade in hosted mode.

SCOPE:
- Extend `/api/health` or add `/api/capabilities` with a typed capability shape including at least: `deploymentMode: 'local' | 'desktop' | 'web'`, `canStartRuns`, `canDryRun`, `canOpenLocalFiles`, `canStreamRunEvents`, and `requiresAuth`.
- Update shared dashboard types and Query hook data so UI can branch on capabilities.
- Gate local-only UI actions such as `ArtifactActions` / `/api/files/open` behind `canOpenLocalFiles`.
- Preserve local CLI behavior when capabilities say file opening is allowed.
- In hosted/web mode, disable or hide local file opening while keeping prompt/run detail content readable.

ACCEPTANCE:
- Capability payload is available through Query-backed state.
- Local-only buttons are capability-gated with clear disabled states or safe alternatives.
- Hosted mode has no unconditional local filesystem actions.
- Unit/component tests cover capability-gated artifact actions.
- `npm run dashboard:build` passes.
close_reason: Health capabilities added and local-only file/run actions gated for local/desktop/web deployment modes; server and route tests pass.

title: Make dashboard API transport configurable for local desktop and hosted web
description:
WHY: Local CLI can use same-origin `/api`, but desktop and hosted web deployments may need a configured API origin and different auth. The transport layer should be portable without route components knowing deployment details.

SCOPE:
- Keep `src/dashboard/web/src/api.ts` as the typed transport layer, but add a configurable API base URL path that works for local same-origin, desktop loopback, and hosted web API origins.
- Preserve current token bootstrap behavior during migration, but avoid designing new routes around URL token assumptions.
- Ensure auth headers are applied in one place.
- Document how API base/auth should be provided for local, desktop, and web deployments.
- Keep Query hooks/mutations as the only callers most components touch.

ACCEPTANCE:
- API calls can target same-origin or configured base URL without changing component code.
- Existing local dashboard still works.
- Hosted mode can be configured without hardcoded localhost assumptions.
- Tests cover URL construction/auth header behavior where practical.
close_reason: API client resolves same-origin, window, or meta-tag API bases and keeps auth/header handling centralized; documented local/desktop/web modes.

title: Install TanStack Router and add initial dashboard route shell
description:
WHY: Router should be introduced as a thin route spine, not a full app rewrite. The first Router commit should preserve the existing shell and Query provider while route params start replacing local selection state.

SCOPE:
- Add `@tanstack/react-router` and create `src/dashboard/web/src/router.tsx`.
- Wrap the dashboard app with `RouterProvider` while preserving `QueryClientProvider` access to the same `dashboardQueryClient`.
- Add initial route shells for `/workflows/$workflowId` and `/runs/$runId`.
- Keep existing UI layout and behavior intact.
- Read route params in the app shell while temporarily mirroring them into existing state as needed.
- Keep `?workflow=<id>` compatibility by redirecting or normalizing to the workflow route.

ACCEPTANCE:
- `/workflows/<id>` and `/runs/<id>` load the existing dashboard shell.
- Existing sidebar/recent-run workflows still work.
- Browser refresh on a route preserves the intended workflow/run selection.
- `npm run dashboard:build` passes.
close_reason: Added @tanstack/react-router, route shell, RouterProvider, route parser, and legacy workflow URL normalization; dashboard build passes.

title: Route workflow selection and step Inspector state
description:
WHY: Workflow definition browsing is distinct from run inspection. In workflow routes, card clicks should select a step for the right Inspector, and agent pills should toggle model selection. These semantics should come from route mode, not inferred scattered local state.

SCOPE:
- Move workflow selection from `selectedWorkflowId`/manual URL updates into `/workflows/$workflowId` params.
- Add `/workflows/$workflowId/steps/$stepId` for Inspector step selection.
- Make `WorkflowList` render route links/navigation rather than button-only local state selection.
- Drive `useWorkflowGraphQuery(workflowId)` from route params.
- Derive selected node from the workflow step route when present.
- Ensure `WorkflowCanvas` remains in `configure` mode for workflow routes.

ACCEPTANCE:
- `/workflows/review` opens Review in configure mode.
- `/workflows/review/steps/cross-review` opens Review with Cross Review selected in Inspector.
- Card click navigates/selects the step without opening run details.
- Agent pill click toggles model selection.
- Back/forward works between selected steps.
- `npm run dashboard:build` passes.
close_reason: Workflow id and step selection now come from /workflows routes; card clicks navigate to step routes and Inspector derives selected node from route.

title: Route WorkflowPromptModal prompt sequence and prompt step state
description:
WHY: `WorkflowPromptModal` is read-only, identity-based, and answers an important share/debug question: what exactly will this workflow send to the agents? It should not remain local-only `promptModalStepId` state.

SCOPE:
- Add `/workflows/$workflowId/prompts` to open the prompt sequence at the first prompt.
- Add `/workflows/$workflowId/prompts/$stepId` to deep-link to a step prompt.
- Replace `promptModalStepId` with route params.
- Make the "View workflow prompts" control navigate to the selected step prompt when a step is selected, otherwise to the prompt sequence route.
- Make timeline selection inside `WorkflowPromptModal` navigate to prompt-step routes, using replace instead of push if back-stack noise is too high.
- Source prompt entries from the workflow graph/query response and selected workflow; do not read browser filesystem directly.
- Capability-gate local file actions in prompt metadata.

ACCEPTANCE:
- `/workflows/review/prompts` opens the prompt sequence.
- `/workflows/review/prompts/cross-review` opens directly to Cross Review prompt.
- Modal close returns to the workflow or selected step route.
- Timeline selection updates route and active prompt.
- Hosted-web capability mode can view prompts without local file opening.
- Component/e2e tests cover deep-link and close behavior.
- `npm run dashboard:build` passes.
close_reason: WorkflowPromptModal now opens from /workflows/:id/prompts routes, deep-links prompt steps, updates timeline selection via route replace, and gates file-open actions.

title: Route run selection and run graph inspection mode
description:
WHY: Recent run selection currently mutates local state and loads run graph data imperatively. Run inspection should be deep-linkable and clearly set the graph to inspect mode.

SCOPE:
- Move selected run identity from `selectedRunId` local state into `/runs/$runId` route params.
- Make `RecentRuns` navigate to `/runs/$runId`.
- Use `queryClient.setQueryData` opportunistically for the selected run, then let `useRunGraphQuery(runId, { refetchActiveGraphs: true })` load/refresh the graph.
- Ensure selecting a workflow route exits run-inspection mode.
- Preserve live event reducer behavior for active runs.

ACCEPTANCE:
- `/runs/<runId>` opens the run graph in inspect mode.
- Recent run click navigates to the run route.
- Card clicks on run routes open details; agent pills open agent-specific details once details routes land.
- Active run polling/event behavior remains intact.
- `npm run dashboard:build` passes.
close_reason: Run selection now comes from /runs routes; RecentRuns navigates, run graph query is route-driven, and workflow routes clear inspect state.

title: Route RunDetailsModal step and agent detail state
description:
WHY: `RunDetailsModal` is already the correct UI for completed/live step and agent details, but its open state is local. Details are shareable and should survive refresh/back/forward.

SCOPE:
- Add `/runs/$runId/steps/$stepId` to open step details.
- Add `/runs/$runId/steps/$stepId/agents/$agent` to open model-specific details.
- Replace `detailsModalContext` with route-derived selector inputs where possible.
- Keep `useRunDetailsQuery(runId)` as the details data source.
- Keep `detailsModalLiveContext` local while an event stream is active, but derive its selector from route params.
- Modal close navigates back to `/runs/$runId`.

ACCEPTANCE:
- Deep link to step details opens the modal after refresh.
- Deep link to agent details opens directly to that agent/model result.
- Modal close updates the URL and preserves loaded run graph/output.
- Switching between run overview and modal routes does not remount/refit the React Flow canvas unnecessarily.
- `npm run dashboard:build` passes.
close_reason: RunDetailsModal context now derives from /runs/:id/steps/:stepId(/agents/:agent), with close returning to run overview and live selectors preserved.

title: Remove legacy local route mirrors and manual history updates
description:
WHY: After route params own workflow/run/step/prompt/detail identity, legacy local mirrors and manual history mutations become bugs waiting to happen.

SCOPE:
- Remove `selectedWorkflowId` once workflow routes provide it.
- Remove `selectedRunId` once run routes provide it.
- Remove `selectedNode` if it can be derived from `/workflows/$workflowId/steps/$stepId`; otherwise keep only ephemeral fallback selection with clear reason.
- Remove `promptModalStepId` after prompt routes land.
- Remove `detailsModalContext` after run details routes land.
- Remove `initialWorkflowFromUrl`, `setWorkflowUrl`, and direct `window.history.replaceState` from dashboard app code except any one-time compatibility redirect.
- Remove local `graph` once canvas data derives from Query results plus `projectWorkflowGraph`.

ACCEPTANCE:
- No manual history mutation remains for normal dashboard navigation.
- Refresh/back/forward behavior is route-driven.
- No workflow definition action opens empty run details.
- `npm run dashboard:build` passes.
close_reason: Removed local route mirrors for workflow/run/node/prompt/details/graph and manual dashboard navigation history writes; route params and Query now own view identity.

title: nax try: offline mock transport for zero-setup engine runs
description:
WHY: The first-run cliff is brutal. Before a user sees anything, nax demands gh auth login, netlify login, nax init, repo secrets, and a linked Netlify site. And the orchestration engine itself (steps, fan-out, waitFor gating, round-results chaining, artifacts, resume) lives in the 7,375-line bin/nax.js with NO direct test coverage.

GOAL: A built-in mock transport plus a nax try command that runs ANY flow end to end against synthesized agent results, instantly, offline, with no gh/Netlify/credits. Two payoffs from one feature: (1) onboarding -- npx ... try shows the whole UX (fan-out, gating, green visualizer graph, .nax artifacts) in ~10 seconds; (2) testability -- a deterministic seam to assert engine orchestration without burning credits or hitting the network. It is also the replay substrate the recorded-transcript harness (nax-mgi) needs.

NON-GOALS: not a fidelity guarantee for real transports (mock tests the ENGINE, not the Netlify/GitHub drivers which shell out to external services); not the full record/replay harness (that is nax-mgi -- this defines a compatible fixture format and an extension point); not a real blob upload (stubbed); not a bin/nax.js monolith split.

RESOLVED DECISIONS (2026-06-20):
D1 Transports adopt a standard Transport interface (detect/submitAgent/waitForStep) that github, netlify-api, and mock all implement, derived from current behavior.
D2 Mock artifacts are sandboxed under .nax/try/ (never real .nax/); recent/visualize/handoff gain an opt-in second search root.
D3 v1 = deterministic synthetic generator (works for any flow) + one recorded review fixture; ships independent of nax-mgi.
D4 Ship nax try sugar + raw --transport mock.

SOURCE: docs/ai/plans/nax-try-mock-transport.md. Came from idea-wizard finalist #3. Relates to nax-mgi (recorded transcripts) and nax-pja (--json contract).

title: Transport interface, registry, and engine dispatch rewire
description:
WHY: The engine hard-branches between two transport drivers at ~4 sites (bin/nax.js:4102, :6430, :6450, :6519) with runState.transport === github conditionals. To plug in a third transport cleanly (decision D1) transports must conform to one standard interface.

WHAT: Define a Transport interface + a getTransport(kind) registry; route the engine through it.
- src/transports/types.js: JSDoc typedef Transport { kind, detect() -> availability+reason, submitAgent(args) -> AgentRun (honors submit new-run vs follow-up), waitForStep(args) -> AgentRun[] (blocks until every fanned-out agent is terminal) }. Keep it LEAN (detect/submit/wait only); grow later only on real need (YAGNI).
- src/transports/index.js: getTransport(kind) returns the impl for github | netlify-api | mock.
- Adapt the two existing drivers via thin behavior-preserving wrappers: github submitAgent=runSingleGithubAgent (bin/nax.js:2311), waitForStep=waitForGithubStep (:5289); netlify submitAgent=runSingleNetlifyAgent (:2141), waitForStep=waitForLocalAgentRuns (local-runner.js:818). Do NOT rewrite driver internals -- wrapping satisfies the interface without touching the risky guts.
- Replace the engine dispatch branches with getTransport(runState.transport).submitAgent/.waitForStep.
- Thread an injectable clock: pass now into createRunState (run-state.js:196 already accepts now) at the call site so determinism hooks can fix it.

ACCEPTANCE: github and netlify-api runs behave identically (existing transport unit tests stay green; a real smoke run unchanged); getTransport(mock) has a ready slot; no remaining transport === string branches in the engine dispatch path.

CONSIDERATIONS: this touches the untested real paths -- the riskiest bead. The mock engine Tests bead must land alongside so the dispatch rewire is covered. Native refactor of driver internals is explicitly OUT OF SCOPE.

title: Recorded review fixture (realistic demo)
description:
WHAT: Ship one realistic recorded review fixture set (claude/gemini/codex across the review flow steps) under tests/fixtures/mock/review/ so nax try review shows a convincing, non-lorem demo while custom flows still fall back to synthetic.
- meta.source=recorded. Sourced from a real run, sanitized: no secrets, no private SHAs.

ACCEPTANCE: nax try review uses the recorded fixtures (not synthetic) and renders a believable multi-agent review + cross-review + consensus in the artifacts and visualizer.

title: Visualizer offline mock run + smoke test
description:
WHAT: Because mock is a real transport, the visualizer (workflow-runner.js re-enters the engine with --transport <kind>, workflow-runner.js:24) can launch a mock run with zero setup. Wire a one-click offline run path and extend the Playwright visualize:smoke test to: start a mock run, assert the graph reaches all-green, and open a resulting artifact.

ACCEPTANCE: visualize:smoke runs fully offline (no gh/netlify) and passes; the graph transitions to completed for every node from the mock lifecycle events.

title: Tests: mock unit + engine integration + failure-path + determinism
description:
SCOPE (TDD -- write these FIRST; they go green as the impl beads land). Framework node:test via node --import tsx --test. Pristine output; intentional failure/timeout fixtures capture and assert their error text.

UNIT: synthetic generator determinism; fixture file override beats synthetic; mock result matches normalizeAgentRunResult shape; status/delayMs honored; transports resolution (mock + aliases + auto-exclusion + always-available).

INTEGRATION (the previously-missing engine coverage): nax try review runs all three steps; assert step gating ORDER, that cross-review sees round-1 output (round-results chaining), and that the sandbox workflow.json + artifacts/summary.md exist with expected structure; assert NO gh/netlify child process spawned. A fixture-less custom flow runs purely on synthetic results.

FAILURE PATHS: status failed -> failure surfaced, downstream still runs on survivors; status timeout -> timeout path; resume from an interrupted mock run continues from the first incomplete step.

DETERMINISM: NAX_FIXED_NOW + fixed seed -> stable workflow.json snapshot.

ACCEPTANCE: all green; coverage spans engine orchestration that bin/nax.js previously had no direct tests for. Detailed logging in test scripts.

title: Docs: README nax try + mock transport + fidelity caveat
description:
WHAT: Document the feature.
- README: new nax try section (zero-setup demo), --transport mock in the transport table, and a clear FIDELITY CAVEAT (mock tests the engine, not the real Netlify/GitHub drivers). Document NAX_MOCK_FIXTURES, NAX_FIXED_NOW, and --from-try / --mock browsing flags.
- Update docs/roadmap-ideas.md to mark nax try delivered.
- Cross-reference nax-qgr (documentation wave umbrella).

ACCEPTANCE: README shows the npx ... try quickstart; the fidelity caveat is explicit; new env knobs and flags are in the reference tables.

title: Extension point: fixture == recording format for nax-mgi
description:
WHAT: Lock the fixture schema as the recording target for the recorded-transcript harness (nax-mgi). Document that nax-mgi.1 (recorder at gh + netlify-api seams) should write the same { schemaVersion, agent, status, delayMs, resultText, usage, links, meta } shape so recorded traffic drops straight into tests/fixtures/mock/ and replays through the mock transport. Provide a small converter stub if the recorder envelope differs.

ACCEPTANCE: a short doc note + a shared/referenced schema constant so nax-mgi can record into mock-replayable fixtures without a second format. Cross-referenced from nax-mgi.1/nax-mgi.2.

title: JSON robot mode for nax try
description:
WHAT: nax try --json emits one stable JSON document on stdout (runId, status, per-step per-agent statuses, artifact paths, usage rollup) with all decorative output on stderr, consistent with the --json contract being defined in nax-pja.3. Align key names (camelCase) with that contract; if nax-pja.3 has not landed, define minimal stable keys here and document them.

ACCEPTANCE: nax try review --json piped to jq works; exactly one JSON doc on stdout; no ANSI codes inside the JSON; non-TTY suppresses spinners/pickers.

title: Register mock transport kind
description:
WHAT: Make mock a real transport kind in src/transports.js.
- Add mock to TRANSPORTS (currently github,netlify-api at transports.js:7) and aliases try/offline/demo -> mock in TRANSPORT_ALIASES (:8).
- detectTransports (:55): mock is ALWAYS available (no prereqs, reason like mock: no setup required) but is NEVER chosen by --transport auto. auto must keep preferring github then netlify-api.
- resolveTransport (:79) validates and resolves mock and its aliases.

ACCEPTANCE: --transport mock and --transport try resolve to mock; --transport auto never returns mock even when github+netlify are absent; mock reported available with a clear reason. Unit tests for resolution + aliases + auto-exclusion.

WHY never-auto: mock must be opt-in only so a misconfigured repo never silently runs fake agents instead of erroring.

title: Mock transport driver
description:
WHAT: src/mock-transport.js implements the Transport interface for kind mock.
- submitAgent({ flowId, stepId, agent, promptText, options }) resolves a fixture (see Fixture system bead) and returns a normalizeAgentRunResult-shaped object (agent-run-results.js:497): { agent, status, resultText, usage, links, runnerId, sessionId, ... }. Synthetic ids are deterministic: mock-<flow>-<step>-<agent>.
- waitForStep(...) resolves immediately by default; honors per-fixture delayMs and status so tests can drive slow, failed, and timeout paths through the engine gating logic.
- Emits the SAME structured lifecycle events (runner-events.js / runner-event-log.js) as real transports so the visualizer graph transitions submitted -> running -> completed.

ACCEPTANCE: a single mock step fans out to N agents and returns N normalized results; status failed and timeout fixtures propagate through waitFor gating; events.jsonl written for the run; NO network or child gh/netlify process spawned.

CONSIDERATIONS: follow-up (submit: follow-up) must reuse the deterministic runnerId so round-results chaining sees a stable thread, mirroring real follow-up sessions.

title: Fixture system: schema, synthetic generator, resolver
description:
WHAT: The data layer mock results come from. Resolution order for (flowId, stepId, agent): (1) explicit fixture file tests/fixtures/mock/<flowId>/<stepId>/<agent>.json (or dir from NAX_MOCK_FIXTURES); (2) deterministic synthetic generator seeded by hash(flowId, stepId, agent, seed).
- Synthetic generator produces a plausible structured result: a few fake file:line findings, a short summary, zero-cost usage, meta.source=synthetic. Deterministic for a fixed seed (critical for snapshot tests and reproducible demos).
- Fixture schema v1 (also the recording target for nax-mgi): { schemaVersion, agent, status (completed|failed|timeout), delayMs, resultText, usage{credits,inputTokens,outputTokens,costUsd}, links[], meta{source} }.
- Resolver: file overrides synthetic; missing file falls back silently to synthetic.

ACCEPTANCE: nax try on a fixture-less custom flow runs entirely on synthetic results; same seed yields byte-identical synthetic output; a present fixture file overrides synthetic. Unit tests for determinism, override precedence, schema validation.

WHY synthetic-default: it is the onboarding promise -- nax try ANY flow works with zero authored files -- and minimizes fixture maintenance burden.

title: Mock auto-context (synthetic, non-git-safe) and blob stub
description:
WHY: buildAutomaticContext (review-context.js:245) shells to git and gh to pin a SHA and build a PR ledger; that breaks the zero-setup promise (must work in a fresh npx dir, possibly not a git repo). Blob offload shells to netlify blobs:set/get.

WHAT:
- In mock mode supply a deterministic SYNTHETIC auto-context (fake pinned SHA, empty/fake PR ledger) so the prompt-assembly path is still exercised, but nothing shells out. --no-auto-context still honored. Never crash when not in a git repo.
- Stub netlify blobs:set/get in mock mode (no network). Still run the offload DECISION (buildAndMaybeFallbackPlan) so size-budget logic is exercised, and still write local debug mirrors under the run blobs/ dir. Mark blob refs mode:mock so nax clean blobs skips remote deletes.

ACCEPTANCE: nax try in an empty non-git tmpdir completes; prompt includes synthetic context; an oversized step still exercises the offload decision and writes a local mirror with no network call.

title: nax try command (sugar, banner, mode:mock, non-git safe)
description:
WHAT: nax try [flow] = nax run [flow] --transport mock --force plus UX sugar.
- Loud banner: MOCK MODE -- no real agents, no credits, no network (with a flask/test marker).
- Default flow review; interactive picker if a TTY and no flow id given.
- Tags the run mode: mock in workflow.json.
- Works outside a git repo and in an empty npx directory.
- On completion prints the sandbox artifact path and the next commands (nax recent --mock, nax visualize <flow>, nax handoff --from-try).
- Register in buildProgram (bin/nax.js:6958) alongside run/visualize/etc.

ACCEPTANCE: npx ... try in a clean dir runs review to completion offline, prints the banner once, and emits the follow-up hints; nax try ./custom-flow runs a custom flow on synthetic results.

title: Sandboxed artifacts under .nax/try/ + naxRoots() resolver
description:
WHY (decision D2): mock runs must never pollute real .nax/ latest symlinks, handoff, or resume.

WHAT:
- Mock runs write to a sandbox root .nax/try/ (workflows/, agent-runners/, agent-sessions/, with a sandbox-local latest symlink). run-state and workflow-artifacts honor the sandbox root for mock runs.
- Introduce naxRoots() returning [.nax] normally and [.nax, .nax/try] when mock browsing is in scope.
- Add .nax/try/ to .gitignore (ephemeral demo data).

ACCEPTANCE: a mock run creates .nax/try/workflows/<id>/... and updates ONLY the sandbox latest; real .nax/latest is untouched; a real run is unaffected; workflow.json carries mode:mock. Unit/integration coverage of root isolation.

title: Second search root in recent/visualize/handoff
description:
WHAT: Teach the browsing/handoff commands about the sandbox root via naxRoots().
- nax recent: include .nax/try only with --mock/--include-mock; tag mock entries with a flask marker so they are never confused with real runs.
- nax visualize: resolve a run id across both roots; render mock runs with a flask badge.
- nax handoff: does NOT include .nax/try by default (handing off fake results is almost never intended); --from-try opts in explicitly.

ACCEPTANCE: nax recent hides mock runs by default, shows them with --mock; nax visualize can open a mock run by id; nax handoff ignores mock runs unless --from-try. Tests for default-hide and opt-in.

title: Determinism hooks (NAX_FIXED_NOW)
description:
WHAT: Make mock runs reproducible for snapshot tests.
- NAX_FIXED_NOW env feeds a fixed clock threaded into createRunState({ now }) (run-state.js:196) so run-id and timestamps are stable.
- Combined with the deterministic synthetic generator, a fixed seed + fixed now yields a byte-stable workflow.json and artifact set (modulo symlink inodes, which snapshots ignore).

ACCEPTANCE: two nax try runs with NAX_FIXED_NOW and a fixed seed produce identical workflow.json content; a snapshot test asserts the stable shape.

title: Visualize Phase 1: read-only React Flow workflow demo
description: Build the first safe demo: `nax visualize [workflow]` starts a local server, opens a Vite 8 + React 19 + React Flow app, lists all workflows from the existing loader, and renders the selected workflow as deterministic step nodes and dependency edges. This phase deliberately avoids remote side effects and real run submission.
close_reason: Completed Phase 1 read-only visualizer: toolchain, graph transform, localhost API, CLI command, Vite React Flow workbench, static packaging, tests, browser smoke, and README documentation.

title: Visualize UI tooling: add Vite 8, React 19, React Flow deps and scripts
description:
WHAT: Add the frontend/tooling dependencies and scripts required for the visualize UI while keeping the existing CommonJS CLI usable. Add root package dependencies/devDependencies for Vite 8, React 19, React DOM 19, @xyflow/react 12, and the React Vite plugin. Add scripts `visualize:dev`, `visualize:build`, and `visualize:preview` pointing at `web/vite.config.mjs`.

WHY: Root dependencies avoid workspace/release churn for the first UI. The UI source remains isolated under `web/`, but a global install can still ship prebuilt assets later.

CONSTRAINTS: Do not raise the published CLI runtime casually. Document that developing/building the UI needs Node 20.19+ or 22.12+ because Vite 8 requires it, while the built CLI should remain installable for the current engine policy if possible. Avoid adding a separate package manager or workspace unless a later bead proves it necessary.

ACCEPTANCE: `npm install` updates lockfile cleanly; `npm run visualize:build` has a real script target once the web app lands; existing `npm run check` and `npm test` remain callable.
close_reason: Added Vite 8, React 19, React Flow, and plugin-react dev tooling plus visualize dev/build/preview scripts.

title: Document experimental nax visualize Phase 1 usage
description:
WHAT: Update README and/or command help with a short experimental `nax visualize` section. Include purpose, prerequisites, example commands, and honest scope: Phase 1 is read-only workflow visualization, not workflow editing or browser-triggered remote execution.

DETAILS: Mention `nax visualize review`, `--no-open`, `--project-root`, and the dev/build scripts. Note that UI development/building uses Vite 8 and therefore needs Node 20.19+ or 22.12+, while published built assets should avoid requiring Vite at runtime.

WHY: Users should understand what the command does and does not do, especially before run buttons are introduced.

ACCEPTANCE: README has a concise section matching the existing direct style. Help text marks the command experimental if that remains the product decision.
close_reason: Added README command entry and experimental nax visualize section covering examples, read-only Phase 1 scope, workflow loader behavior, no side effects, and Vite/Node build requirement.

title: Add browser smoke coverage for React Flow visualizer
description:
WHAT: Add browser smoke/e2e coverage for the Phase 1 UI once the app can build. Start the visualize server against a temp project, open the UI in a real browser automation tool or lightweight equivalent, verify workflows load, select Review, assert the canvas is nonblank, assert three nodes/two edges are visible, and capture desktop plus narrow viewport screenshots for debugging.

DETAILS: The test should catch blank React Flow canvases, broken asset serving, text overlap severe enough to hide content, and failure to load selected workflow from URL. Keep this as smoke coverage, not exhaustive visual regression.

WHY: React Flow issues often fail visually even when API tests pass. A real browser smoke test is the guardrail that `nax visualize review` actually produces a usable UI.

ACCEPTANCE: CI or local test script can run the smoke test with detailed logs. On failure it leaves screenshots or clear console output. The test verifies desktop and mobile-ish/narrow layouts.
close_reason: Added Playwright smoke test that starts the visualize server, opens Review, verifies three workflow nodes and two React Flow edges on desktop and narrow viewports, and captures screenshots as attachments.

title: Build pure flow-to-React-Flow graph transform
description:
WHAT: Implement `src/visualize-graph.js`, a pure CommonJS transform that converts a normalized Nax flow into React Flow-compatible nodes, edges, and metadata. Inputs should support `{ flow, selectedAgents, runState }`; output should include stable `nodes`, `edges`, and `metadata`.

DETAILS: Node data must carry `kind: workflow-step`, `stepId`, index, title, description, action, submit, waitFor, agents, input, status, and sourceLabel. Edges come from explicit `step.input[].step` references first. If a later step has no explicit input and the flow is linear, add a sequential edge from the previous runnable step. Use stable edge ids like `edge:<sourceStepId>:<targetStepId>`. Mark follow-up edges or nodes so the UI can visually distinguish follow-up sessions from new agent runs.

WHY: The graph transform is the reusable contract between the CLI/server and React Flow. Keeping it pure makes it easy to unit test, easy to reuse in JSON APIs, and independent from any future layout engine.

ACCEPTANCE: Review flow produces 3 nodes and 2 edges. Explicit `input` dependencies override sequential assumptions. Agent filtering updates node agents without mutating the original flow. The function is deterministic for stable snapshot tests.
close_reason: Implemented pure flowToGraph transform with agent filtering, run-state overlay, deterministic layout, input/sequential edges, and transitive edge reduction; added focused unit coverage.

title: Add read-only localhost visualize API server
description:
WHAT: Add `src/visualize-server.js` and any small API helper module needed to serve read-only visualize data over a localhost HTTP server. Initial routes: `GET /api/health`, `GET /api/workflows`, `GET /api/workflows/:id`, and `GET /api/workflows/:id/graph`.

DETAILS: Use Node built-in `http` initially. Resolve workflows through existing `listFlows()`/`loadFlow()` using the same projectRoot/flowsDir semantics as `nax list`. Return stable JSON errors with status, code, and message. Bind to `127.0.0.1` by default. Generate a per-process token and include it in server metadata even though Phase 1 has no mutating endpoints, so the security shape is ready before run buttons exist.

WHY: The UI must not duplicate filesystem/config loading in browser code. The local server is the boundary where CLI-aware project context becomes browser-safe JSON.

ACCEPTANCE: A test can start the server on an ephemeral port, fetch health, list workflows, and fetch the Review graph. Unknown workflow ids return structured 404 JSON. Project-local flows appear before bundled flows, matching existing loader behavior.
close_reason: Added read-only localhost visualize API server with health, workflows, workflow detail, and workflow graph routes; live probe returns Review graph as 3 nodes and 2 edges.

title: Add nax visualize [workflow] command and server lifecycle
description:
WHAT: Add `nax visualize [workflow]` to Commander wiring. Options: `--project-root <path>`, repeatable `--flows-dir <path>`, `--host <host>` default `127.0.0.1`, `--port <port>` default auto, `--no-open`, and `--dev` for local Vite development integration.

DETAILS: Resolve project root with the same helper as list/run. Start the visualize server, open the browser using the existing `open` dependency unless `--no-open` is set, encode the initial workflow id in the URL, print URL and project root, and keep the process alive until interrupted. Do not run workflow execution in Phase 1.

WHY: This is the user-facing entry point and should feel like a natural Nax command, not a separate web app the user needs to discover.

ACCEPTANCE: `node bin/nax.js visualize review --no-open` starts the server and prints a local URL without opening a browser. Invalid initial workflow ids surface clearly. `--flows-dir` and `--project-root` affect the workflow list the same way they do for `nax list`.
close_reason: Added nax visualize [workflow] command with project-root, flows-dir, host, port, no-open, and dev options; spawned smoke confirms URL/project-root output.

title: Create Vite React workbench shell and API client
description:
WHAT: Create the Vite application under `web/` with `index.html`, `vite.config.mjs`, TypeScript config if used, `src/main.tsx`, `src/App.tsx`, API client, shared types, and the operational workbench shell.

LAYOUT: Left sidebar for workflow list/search/source labels, main React Flow canvas, right inspector, top toolbar for project root/refresh/future run controls, and bottom/status area reserved for future dry-run/run streams. The first viewport should be the actual workbench, not a hero or documentation page.

WHY: The initial UI should prove the product direction immediately: workflows are selectable, graph-shaped, and inspectable.

ACCEPTANCE: The app fetches `/api/workflows`, selects the URL workflow or first workflow, handles loading/error/empty states, and does not require any remote service. `npm run visualize:build` compiles once the app and dependencies are present.
close_reason: Created Vite React workbench shell, API client, URL workflow selection, loading/error states, toolbar, and status strip.

title: Implement React Flow canvas and custom workflow step node
description:
WHAT: Implement the React Flow canvas and custom workflow step node using `@xyflow/react` named imports and `@xyflow/react/dist/style.css`. Include `ReactFlowProvider`, `ReactFlow`, `Background`, `Controls`, and optionally `MiniMap` if it helps orientation.

NODE DESIGN: Header shows step number/title and action label. Body shows description. Footer shows agent chips. Include top target and bottom source handles for edge attachment, with handle dots visually hidden by CSS but still present. Use `nodesDraggable={false}`, `nodesConnectable={false}`, selectable elements, `fitView`, and smoothstep default edges for Phase 1.

WHY: This is the visual equivalent of the existing terminal box preview, but with native graph interaction and a path to future live state overlays.

ACCEPTANCE: Review renders as three clear nodes connected vertically. Long titles/descriptions wrap without overlap. Selecting a node updates inspector state. Canvas is nonblank and usable at desktop and narrow widths.
close_reason: Implemented React Flow canvas with custom workflow step nodes, hidden handles, smoothstep edges, controls, minimap, fitView, and responsive styling.

title: Build workflow list, search, and step/workflow inspector
description:
WHAT: Build workflow list, search/filter, and inspector components. The list should show workflow title, id, source label, step count, and model summary. The inspector should show selected workflow metadata by default and selected step metadata when a node is selected.

DETAILS: Reuse normalized fields from the API: description, defaults, source/sourceLabel, steps, agents, action, submit, waitFor, input, prompt path when exposed. Keep the UI dense and operational. Use icon buttons for refresh/open-type commands when practical.

WHY: A graph alone is not enough; operators need to compare workflows, see where a project-local flow came from, and inspect exactly what a selected step will do.

ACCEPTANCE: Search narrows the workflow list. Source labels distinguish project vs bundled workflows. Selecting a workflow refreshes graph and inspector. Selecting a step shows action, submit mode, agents, input dependencies, waitFor, and description.
close_reason: Implemented searchable workflow list and inspector for workflow/step metadata including source labels, step actions, submit modes, agents, inputs, and paths.

title: Serve built UI assets and include them in package files
description:
WHAT: Wire production static asset serving and npm package inclusion for the visualize UI. The server should serve `web/dist` in normal mode after `npm run visualize:build`; dev mode can point operators at the Vite dev server or proxy to it.

DETAILS: Update package `files` so built UI assets are included in published tarballs. Ensure the server can find assets relative to installed package paths, not only the source checkout. Add a clear error when assets are missing that tells developers to run `npm run visualize:build`.

WHY: Global `nax visualize` should work for users without asking them to install Vite or run a dev server.

ACCEPTANCE: After build, `nax visualize --no-open` serves `index.html` and static assets from `web/dist`. A packed npm artifact would include the needed files. Missing assets fail with a helpful message rather than a blank browser.
close_reason: Built UI assets under web/dist, added static serving from visualize server, verified asset responses, and included web/dist in package files.

title: Add Phase 1 graph and read-only API tests
description:
WHAT: Add focused tests for Phase 1: graph transform unit tests and read-only visualize API integration tests.

CASES: Review graph has 3 nodes/2 edges; explicit input dependencies generate correct edges; sequential fallback works when expected; agent filtering changes node data; project-local workflow metadata survives API serialization; unknown workflow id returns structured 404; server health route returns project root/version-like metadata; no mutating endpoint exists yet.

WHY: The UI depends on data correctness more than pixel perfection in Phase 1. The pure graph transform and API routes are where regressions are easiest to catch cheaply.

ACCEPTANCE: Tests run under existing `node --test` patterns. Failures print the graph/API payloads needed to debug. `npm test` and `npm run check` pass.
close_reason: Added graph transform and visualize server tests covering Review graph, sequential fallback, agent filtering, run-state overlay, health/list/detail/graph routes, project flow precedence, 404 errors, and static asset serving.
